subject: Surgery
front: In ambulatory surgery, how is day/same-day surgery defined?
back: Patient admitted and discharged within 12 hours.

subject: Surgery
front: What does 'overnight stay' mean in ambulatory surgery?
back: 23-hour admission with early-morning discharge.

subject: Surgery
front: How is short-stay surgery defined?
back: Admission up to 72 hours.

subject: Surgery
front: Which ASA grades are eligible for a standalone day care unit?
back: ASA grade 1 and 2.

subject: Surgery
front: Where must ASA class 3 patients have day care surgery?
back: In integrated centres.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the BP cut-off for day care surgery eligibility?
back: BP < 180/100 mmHg.

subject: Surgery
front: What HbA1C cut-off applies for a diabetic in day care surgery, and what dose adjustment is made?
back: HbA1C < 8.5; skip the morning dose of OHA.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the eligible BMI cut-off for surface procedures in day care surgery?
back: BMI < 40 kg/m2.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the eligible BMI cut-off for laparoscopic procedures in day care surgery?
back: BMI < 38 kg/m2.

subject: Surgery
front: Are epilepsy patients eligible for day care surgery?
back: Yes, if it is a well-controlled case of epilepsy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication after day care surgery?
back: Post-operative nausea and vomiting.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication requiring re-admission after day care surgery?
back: Haemorrhage.

subject: Surgery
front: Name a third common post-operative complication of day care surgery (besides nausea/vomiting and haemorrhage).
back: Pain.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the vital-sign discharge criterion after day care surgery?
back: Vital signs stable for at least 1 hour, and patient oriented to time, place, person.

subject: Surgery
front: What pain-control criterion must be met for discharge?
back: Adequate pain control with oral analgesia.

subject: Surgery
front: What intake/output criterion is needed for discharge?
back: Has taken oral fluids and has passed urine (if appropriate).

subject: Surgery
front: What wound criterion is needed for discharge?
back: Minimal wound discharge/bleeding.

subject: Surgery
front: What nausea criterion is needed for discharge?
back: Minimal nausea.

subject: Surgery
front: What does ERAS stand for?
back: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: Name the preoperative counselling component of ERAS.
back: Patient counselling.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is mechanical bowel preparation avoided in ERAS?
back: Because it causes fluid and electrolyte imbalance.

subject: Surgery
front: How long before surgery may solids be taken under ERAS?
back: Up to 6 hours before surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: How long before surgery may clear carbohydrate-rich liquids be taken under ERAS?
back: Up to 2 hours before surgery (carbohydrate loading).

subject: Surgery
front: What surgical approach is preferred intra-operatively in ERAS?
back: Minimally invasive.

subject: Surgery
front: Which long-acting local anaesthetic is used in ERAS and why?
back: Long-acting local bupivacaine, to reduce post-op pain.

subject: Surgery
front: Name two intra-operative ERAS measures regarding temperature and nausea.
back: Keep the patient warm; give nausea + vomiting prophylaxis (at least 2 classes of medications).

subject: Surgery
front: Which intra-operative devices are avoided in ERAS?
back: Intra-operative drains and Ryle's tube.

subject: Surgery
front: What analgesia approach is used post-operatively in ERAS?
back: Use NSAIDs and avoid opioids.

subject: Surgery
front: List the three things done within 24 hours post-op in ERAS.
back: Discontinue IV fluids, start a regular diet, and ambulate.

subject: Surgery
front: What gauge is the violet IV cannula?
back: 26 G (flow rate not specified).

subject: Surgery
front: What gauge and maximal flow rate has the yellow IV cannula?
back: 24 G, 13 mL/min.

subject: Surgery
front: What gauge and maximal flow rate has the blue IV cannula?
back: 22 G, 31 mL/min.

subject: Surgery
front: What gauge and maximal flow rate has the pink IV cannula?
back: 20 G, 67 mL/min.

subject: Surgery
front: What gauge and maximal flow rate has the green IV cannula?
back: 18 G, 103 mL/min.

subject: Surgery
front: What gauge and maximal flow rate has the gray IV cannula?
back: 16 G, 236 mL/min.

subject: Surgery
front: What gauge and maximal flow rate has the orange IV cannula?
back: 14 G, 270 mL/min.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication of cannula insertion?
back: Superficial thrombophlebitis.

subject: Surgery
front: How does superficial thrombophlebitis present?
back: Cord-like tender swelling at the site.

subject: Surgery
front: When is the 'Sign in' phase of the surgical safety checklist done?
back: Before induction of anaesthesia (Ward to OT).

subject: Surgery
front: What three identifiers are confirmed at 'Sign in'?
back: Patient identity, site, and procedure.

subject: Surgery
front: What consent and marking steps are part of 'Sign in'?
back: Take written consent and mark the surgical site.

subject: Surgery
front: What allergy and monitoring checks are part of 'Sign in'?
back: Inquire about allergies and confirm the pulse oximeter is functioning.

subject: Surgery
front: What risk is evaluated at 'Sign in'?
back: Risk of aspiration.

subject: Surgery
front: When is the 'Time out' phase done?
back: Before skin incision.

subject: Surgery
front: What is confirmed during 'Time out'?
back: Team members introduction; confirmation of patient, site and procedure name.

subject: Surgery
front: What two items does the surgeon confirm during 'Time out'?
back: Operative duration and anticipated blood loss.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the anaesthetist confirm during 'Time out'?
back: Antibiotic prophylaxis.

subject: Surgery
front: When is the 'Sign out' phase done?
back: Before the patient leaves.

subject: Surgery
front: What counts and labelling are confirmed at 'Sign out'?
back: Gauze + instrument count and specimen labelling.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the anaesthetist confirm at 'Sign out'?
back: Actual blood loss.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most commonly used OT position and its uses?
back: Supine; used for abdominal and breast surgeries.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the Trendelenburg position and its use.
back: Head end down, foot end up; used for pelvic surgeries.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the reverse Trendelenburg position and its uses.
back: Head end up, foot end down; used for upper abdominal surgeries (e.g. laparoscopic cholecystectomy, bariatric surgery).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of the lithotomy position?
back: Obstetric, gynaecological, and urological procedures.

subject: Surgery
front: Which nerve injury can occur in lithotomy position, and why?
back: Common peroneal nerve injury, if the legs are not properly supported.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of the lateral/kidney position?
back: Thoracotomy and kidney surgeries (e.g. nephrectomy).

subject: Surgery
front: Which nerve injury can occur in lateral/kidney position, and why?
back: Brachial plexus injury, due to overabduction of the arms.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of the prone position?
back: Spinal surgeries and pilonidal sinus surgeries.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of the sitting/Fowler's position?
back: Posterior cranial fossa procedures.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the advantage of the sitting/Fowler's position?
back: Better exposure.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the disadvantage of the sitting/Fowler's position?
back: Increased risk of air embolism.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is the jack-knife position not preferred?
back: Due to positional asphyxia.

subject: Surgery
front: What volume of air causes air embolism?
back: 50 to 100 cc of air.

subject: Surgery
front: In which clinical scenarios does air embolism occur?
back: During thyroid/head and neck surgery (vein nicked, sudden desaturation) and while operating in the sitting position.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of suspected air embolism?
back: Durant's position / left lateral (right side up) with legs up.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is Durant's position used in air embolism?
back: Air remains in the right side of the heart, allowing easy aspiration (via central line/image guided).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of the No. 11 (pointed/stab) blade?
back: Incision and drainage, and arteriotomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the use of the No. 12 (curved) blade?
back: Suture removal.

subject: Surgery
front: What are blades No. 10, 15, 20, 21, 22, 23 (blades with a belly) used for?
back: Making incisions.

subject: Surgery
front: How are surgical blades passed to prevent injuries?
back: Passed in a kidney tray.

subject: Surgery
front: On what handle are surgical blades mounted?
back: Bard Parker (BP) handle.

subject: Surgery
front: What are Langer's lines?
back: Relaxed tension lines of the skin.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the orientation of Langer's lines relative to underlying muscle?
back: Perpendicular to the action of the underlying muscle.

subject: Surgery
front: How should incisions be made relative to Langer's lines, and why?
back: Parallel to the lines, giving minimal scarring.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the current flow in monopolar cautery.
back: Tip -> Body -> Cautery pad -> Machine.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the role of the cautery pad in monopolar cautery, and what happens if it is small/improperly placed?
back: It is required for the cautery to work; if small/improperly placed it causes burns at the site.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the disadvantages of monopolar cautery?
back: Thermal damage to nearby nerves & vital structures (avoid near end arteries); interference with cardiac conduction (avoid in patients with pacemakers).

subject: Surgery
front: What functions can monopolar cautery perform?
back: It can both cut and coagulate.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the current flow in bipolar cautery.
back: Prong 1 -> Body -> Prong 2.

subject: Surgery
front: Does bipolar cautery require a cautery pad?
back: No cautery pad is required.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the advantages of bipolar cautery?
back: Safe to use with pacemakers and can be used near vital structures.

subject: Surgery
front: In which surgeries is bipolar cautery used?
back: Thyroid, parotid, and penile surgeries.

subject: Surgery
front: What function can bipolar cautery perform?
back: Only coagulate.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the waveform of cautery cutting mode.
back: Low voltage, continuous.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the waveform of cautery coagulation mode.
back: High voltage, alternating current.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the working principle of the harmonic scalpel?
back: Ultrasonic coagulation without heat production, using an oscillatory blade (20,000-50,000 Hz oscillation).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the advantages of the harmonic scalpel?
back: Precise cut, can cut through scar tissue, and can be used close to vital structures.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the corrugated rubber drain used for and how common is it?
back: Used in pus cavities; rarely used (an open drain).

subject: Surgery
front: How does the Romovac suction drain work and where is it used?
back: Uses negative pressure; used after mastectomy, thyroidectomy and neck dissection.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Mini-vac drain?
back: A smaller version of the Romovac drain.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the Jackson Pratt drain.
back: Works on negative pressure; has flat tubing and a bulb instead of a bag (a closed drain).

subject: Surgery
front: Does the abdominal drain use negative pressure?
back: No negative pressure.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the under water seal bag drain.
back: Connected to chest tubes, with the end of the tube submerged under water.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the square/reef knot.
back: A secure knot with 2 throws.

subject: Surgery
front: What is characteristic of the Granny's/slip knot?
back: It is not secure and opens up.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the surgeon's knot.
back: 2 throws followed by a single throw.

subject: Surgery
front: How do the crossings of a reef knot compare with a granny knot?
back: In the reef/square knot the crossings are opposite (above-below, below-above); in the granny/slip knot they are not.

subject: Surgery
front: At what angle should the needle enter the skin?
back: 90 degrees with the skin.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the bite taken on each side of the wound?
back: X (where X = depth of the wound).

subject: Surgery
front: What should be the distance between two sutures?
back: 2X (twice the depth of the wound).

subject: Surgery
front: In skin suturing, what does 'X' represent?
back: The depth of the wound.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a characteristic effect of mattress sutures?
back: They are hemostatic sutures and cause eversion of the wound edges.

subject: Surgery
front: Why are subcuticular sutures preferred, and what material is used?
back: Cosmetically better; material used is Monocryl.

subject: Surgery
front: List the uses of purse-string sutures.
back: Rectal prolapse surgery (Thiersch wiring), cervical cerclage, and burying the appendicular stump.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the swaged end of a surgical needle?
back: The end of the needle where the thread is attached.

subject: Surgery
front: How should a surgical needle be held?
back: At 1/3rd from the swaged end and 2/3rd from the pointed end.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the round body (RB) needle and its uses.
back: Rounded cross-section; used for delicate structures (all B's): bowel, bladder, common bile duct (CBD), blood vessels.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the cutting/reverse-cutting needle and its uses.
back: Triangular cross-section; used for tough structures (all S's): skin, sheath, fascia, scalp.

subject: Surgery
front: Which suture number is the thickest?
back: No. 1 suture.

subject: Surgery
front: Which suture number is the finest?
back: No. 11-0 suture.

subject: Surgery
front: Is a thick suture easier or harder to handle?
back: Easier to handle.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a drawback of finer sutures?
back: Difficult to handle (breaks more common).

subject: Surgery
front: From what is catgut derived?
back: Sheep submucosa.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe chromic catgut: coating, absorption mechanism/time, and tissue-holding time.
back: Coated with chromic salts; absorbed by enzymatic degradation over 90 days; holds tissue for 20-21 days.

subject: Surgery
front: What tissue reaction is associated with natural sutures?
back: Increased tissue reaction.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Monocryl/Poliglecaprone suture and its use.
back: Monofilament; used in subcuticular suturing.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Vicryl/Polyglactin: filament type, infection risk, absorption, uses.
back: Braided (increased infection risk); absorbed by hydrolysis over 60-90 days; used for bowel, bladder and CBD.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe PDS suture: filament type, absorption time, uses.
back: Monofilament; absorption over 180 days; same uses as Vicryl.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the tissue-reactivity property of synthetic sutures?
back: They are inert.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of silk suture?
back: Skin suturing and drain fixture (with a cutting needle).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Prolene/Polypropylene suture and its uses.
back: Monofilament; used for rectus sheath closure (suture length = 4x wound length), vascular repair, and as mesh material.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of nylon suture?
back: Skin, nerves, tendons, drain fixture, and cataract surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: When are non-absorbable scalp sutures removed?
back: At 5-7 days.

subject: Surgery
front: When are non-absorbable face sutures removed?
back: At 3-5 days.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the strongest layer in bowel anastomosis?
back: Submucosa.

subject: Surgery
front: What type of edge apposition is used in bowel anastomosis?
back: Inverted edges suturing.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the three methods of bowel anastomosis?
back: Single-layer extra-mucosal, two-layer, and using staplers (all with similar results).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of a linear stapler?
back: Bowel anastomosis, sleeve gastrectomy, and Zenker's diverticulum surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of a circular stapler?
back: Hemorrhoid surgery and low anterior resection (LAR).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of fever on post-op day 1?
back: Atelectasis.

subject: Surgery
front: How is post-op atelectasis prevented?
back: Chest physiotherapy (incentive spirometer), pain control, steam inhalation, and cessation of smoking 4-6 weeks before surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of fever on post-op days 2-3?
back: Superficial thrombophlebitis, UTI, and pneumonia.

subject: Surgery
front: On which post-op days does surgical site infection (SSI) typically appear?
back: Day 4-5.

subject: Surgery
front: How is SSI defined in terms of timing?
back: Wound infection within 30 days of surgery; if post-implantation, within 1 year of surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: Name the two SSI scoring systems.
back: ASEPSIS score and Southampton wound score.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the ASEPSIS score acronym stand for?
back: Additional treatment, Serous discharge, Erythema, Purulent exudate, Separation of deep tissues, Isolation of bacteria, and Prolonged Stay > 14 days.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Southampton wound score grade 0?
back: Normal healing.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Southampton wound score grade I?
back: Normal healing with mild bruising/erythema.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Southampton wound score grade II?
back: Erythema plus other inflammatory signs.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Southampton wound score grade III?
back: Clear discharge.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Southampton wound score grade IV?
back: Pus discharge.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Southampton wound score grade V?
back: Deep/severe wound infection with or without tissue breakdown.

subject: Surgery
front: On which post-op days does deep venous thrombosis typically occur?
back: Day 4-5.

subject: Surgery
front: How is post-op DVT prevented?
back: Pneumatic compression stockings and LMWH.

subject: Surgery
front: What feature is noted on the anti-shock garment?
back: It has numbering.

subject: Surgery
front: On which post-op day does burst abdomen / abdominal wound dehiscence usually occur?
back: Usually on post-op day (POD) 6.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of burst abdomen?
back: Rectus sheath opens up, with a salmon fluid sign / serous fluid sign (serous fluid oozes out).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the emergency management of burst abdomen?
back: Urobag laparostomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the definitive management of burst abdomen?
back: Resuturing of the rectus sheath.

subject: Surgery
front: List the patient factors predisposing to burst abdomen.
back: Chronic cough, constipation, infection, obesity, immunocompromised state, and malnourishment.

subject: Surgery
front: List the surgeon/surgery factors predisposing to burst abdomen.
back: Midline > transverse incision, emergency surgery, continuous > interrupted sutures, large bite > short bite (ideal 0.5 cm), and short thread > long thread (minimum 4 times the length preferred).

subject: Surgery
front: On which post-op days does an intra-abdominal abscess usually occur?
back: Usually POD 7-8.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common site of intra-abdominal abscess overall (and in an ambulatory patient)?
back: Pelvis / pouch of Douglas.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common site of intra-abdominal abscess in a supine patient?
back: Morrison's / hepatorenal pouch.

subject: Surgery
front: How does an intra-abdominal abscess present?
back: Fever with chills and rigors.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for intra-abdominal abscess?
back: CECT.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of an intra-abdominal abscess?
back: Drainage with a pigtail catheter.

subject: Surgery
front: Give examples of a clean wound.
back: Thyroid surgery, knee replacement, breast surgery, CABG, and uncomplicated inguinal hernia surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the SSI rate for clean wounds?

subject: Surgery
front: Define a clean-contaminated wound.
back: Entering the GI/GU system but with no inflammation.

subject: Surgery
front: Give examples of clean-contaminated wounds.
back: Elective/interval cholecystectomy, elective appendectomy, LSCS, laparoscopic abdominal hysterectomy, and bowel surgery if the bowel is prepared.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the SSI rate of clean-contaminated wounds with vs without antibiotic prophylaxis?
back: 3% with prophylaxis; 6-9% without.

subject: Surgery
front: Define a contaminated wound.
back: Entering the GI/GU system with non-purulent inflammation.

subject: Surgery
front: Give examples of contaminated wounds.
back: Emergency cholecystectomy, emergency appendectomy, bowel opened while operating intestinal obstruction, and breach in sterile protocol (e.g. open cardiac massage).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the SSI rate of contaminated wounds with vs without antibiotic prophylaxis?
back: 6% with prophylaxis; 20% without.

subject: Surgery
front: Define a dirty wound.
back: Pus is present.

subject: Surgery
front: Give examples of dirty wounds.
back: All abscesses and peritonitis/fecal contamination.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the SSI rate of dirty wounds with vs without antibiotic prophylaxis?
back: 7-8% with prophylaxis; 20-40% without.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the minimum duration of surgical hand hygiene?
back: Minimum 2-3 minutes.

subject: Surgery
front: Which areas are commonly missed while hand washing?
back: Thumb, inter-digital areas, and finger tips.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the '2 Before' moments of hand hygiene?
back: Before touching a patient, and before a clean/aseptic procedure.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the '3 After' moments of hand hygiene?
back: After body-fluid exposure risk, after touching a patient, and after touching the patient's surroundings.

subject: Surgery
front: When is soap and water preferred over sanitizer?
back: After a toilet visit and when hands are visibly soiled.

subject: Surgery
front: What is used for hair removal before surgery?
back: A hair clipper.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is shaving avoided before surgery?
back: It increases infection rates.

subject: Surgery
front: Which agents are used to clean the parts before surgery?
back: Betadine / alcohol.

subject: Surgery
front: For abdominal surgery, what area is cleaned in males?
back: Nipples to mid-thigh.

subject: Surgery
front: For abdominal surgery, what area is cleaned in females?
back: Inframammary crease to mid-thigh.

subject: Surgery
front: For limb surgery, how far is the area cleaned?
back: Up to 1 joint above.

subject: Surgery
front: When are prophylactic antibiotics ideally given?
back: 30 to 60 minutes before surgery (preferred over giving during induction).

subject: Surgery
front: When is the prophylactic antibiotic dose repeated in prolonged surgery?
back: After 4 hours.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the ideal OT temperature?
back: 18-21 degrees C.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the ideal OT humidity?

subject: Surgery
front: Is there a single reliable biochemical marker for malnutrition?
back: No single reliable biochemical marker exists.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indicators of poor outcome in nutritional assessment?
back: Unintentional weight loss > 10% in 6 months, decreased albumin, and BMI < 15.

subject: Surgery
front: How is body fat assessed?
back: Skin-fold thickness.

subject: Surgery
front: How is muscle mass assessed?
back: Mid-arm circumference.

subject: Surgery
front: What three components make up the MUST score?
back: BMI score + weight-loss score + acute-disease effect score = overall risk of malnutrition.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the two broad types of nutrition support?
back: Enteral (oral/gut) and parenteral (IV route).

subject: Surgery
front: Why is enteral nutrition the preferred route?
back: It is physiological.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best route of enteral nutrition?
back: Oral.

subject: Surgery
front: If oral is not possible for > 3 weeks with good gastric emptying, which route is used?
back: Feeding gastrostomy.

subject: Surgery
front: If not possible for > 3 weeks with poor gastric emptying, which route is used?
back: Feeding jejunostomy.

subject: Surgery
front: If required for < 3 weeks with good gastric emptying, which route is used?
back: Nasogastric (NG) tube / Ryle's tube.

subject: Surgery
front: If required for < 3 weeks with poor gastric emptying, which route is used?
back: Nasojejunal (NJ) tube.

subject: Surgery
front: How is the length of a Ryle's tube assessed?
back: Nose -> Ear -> Xiphisternum.

subject: Surgery
front: How is NG tube position checked?
back: Aspirating gastric contents; and pushing air followed by auscultation in the epigastrium.

subject: Surgery
front: What patient position is used for Ryle's tube insertion?
back: Sitting with the neck slightly flexed.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Stamm method of gastrostomy?
back: Uses a stab incision.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Witzel technique of jejunostomy?
back: Uses a tunnel.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the steps of PEG (percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy) insertion.
back: Endoscope passed into stomach -> illuminated site palpated and punctured -> PEG tube inserted over the guide wire and secured.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication category of enteral nutrition, and give examples.
back: Tube-related (most common): blockade and migration.

subject: Surgery
front: What feeding-regime complications occur with overfeeding in enteral nutrition?
back: Osmotic diarrhoea and increased risk of aspiration (gastrostomy > jejunostomy).

subject: Surgery
front: List the indications for parenteral nutrition.
back: Prolonged paralytic ileus (>72 hours), short bowel syndrome, high-output faecal fistula (>500 cc/24 hours), acute episodes of inflammatory bowel disease, and acute severe pancreatitis (initial phase only).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best method for delivering parenteral nutrition?
back: Central line.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare the subclavian vein route for central line: thrombosis/infection risk, pneumothorax risk, main use.
back: Least thrombosis & infection risk, maximum pneumothorax risk; most commonly used for TPN.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare the internal jugular vein route: thrombosis/infection risk, pneumothorax risk, insertion.
back: Intermediate thrombosis/infection and pneumothorax risk; easiest to insert.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare the femoral vein route: thrombosis/infection risk, pneumothorax risk, main use.
back: Maximum thrombosis & infection risk, least pneumothorax risk; most commonly used in trauma.

subject: Surgery
front: What is done after central line insertion?
back: Chest X-ray to check the central line tip and rule out pneumothorax.

subject: Surgery
front: Where should the PICC line catheter tip lie?
back: In the SVC, just above the right atrium.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the appearance of TPN?
back: Milky fluid.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the protein : fat : carbohydrate ratio in TPN?

subject: Surgery
front: Does TPN contain fibre?
back: No, fibre is absent.

subject: Surgery
front: Besides macronutrients, what does TPN contain?
back: Vitamins, minerals, and micronutrients.

subject: Surgery
front: How is TPN modified in respiratory failure?
back: Decrease carbohydrate and decrease volume.

subject: Surgery
front: How is TPN modified in renal failure?
back: Increase carbohydrate, decrease protein, and decrease quantity.

subject: Surgery
front: What daily clinical measures are monitored on TPN, and what is the earliest sign of overfeeding?
back: Vitals, body weight (increase = earliest sign of overfeeding), and input/output chart.

subject: Surgery
front: What biochemical measures are monitored daily (initially) on TPN?
back: Sodium, potassium, urea, creatinine, blood glucose, magnesium, and phosphate.

subject: Surgery
front: What is monitored weekly on a stable TPN regime?
back: Liver function tests and C-reactive protein.

subject: Surgery
front: List the central-line-related complications of TPN.
back: Pneumothorax, arrhythmias, thrombosis, air embolism, migration, and catheter-induced infection (most common, presenting with fever).

subject: Surgery
front: List the feeding-regime-related complications of TPN.
back: Hyperglycemia (most common), excess weight gain, cholestasis (deranged LFT -> stop TPN), micronutrient deficiency (most common: zinc), and refeeding syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes refeeding syndrome?
back: Large-quantity nutrition given to a chronically malnourished patient.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the main cause of death in refeeding syndrome?
back: Congestive heart failure and arrhythmias.

subject: Surgery
front: What metabolic derangements occur in refeeding syndrome?
back: Decreased K+, decreased Mg2+, decreased Ca2+, decreased PO4 3- (main driver), and fluid overload.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the main driver of refeeding syndrome?
back: Decreased phosphate (PO4 3-).

subject: Surgery
front: List the risk factors for refeeding syndrome.
back: BMI < 16 kg/m2, unintentional weight loss > 15% within last 3-6 months, little/no nutrition intake for > 10 days, and low K+/PO4/Mg2+ prior to feeding.

subject: Surgery
front: How is refeeding syndrome prevented?
back: Increase feeds gradually, strict electrolyte monitoring, and thiamine supplementation.

subject: Surgery
front: What happens to the liver with long-term TPN use?
back: 25% develop liver derangement -> fatty liver -> stop feeds.

subject: Surgery
front: What happens if feeds are continued despite TPN liver derangement?
back: Fibrosis / intestinal failure associated liver disease (IFALD).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the composition of Hartmann's/Ringer Lactate solution in mmol/L.
back: Na+ 131, K+ 5, Ca2+ 2, Cl- 111, Lactate 29.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of shock?
back: Hypovolemic shock.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the sites of concealed hemorrhage?
back: Neck, thorax, abdomen, pelvis, and long bones.

subject: Surgery
front: Does an isolated intracranial bleed cause hypovolemic shock?
back: No, isolated intracranial bleed does not lead to hypovolemic shock.

subject: Surgery
front: When does primary hemorrhage occur?
back: During surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: When does reactionary hemorrhage occur and why?
back: 4-24 hours; due to clot dislodgement or knot slippage.

subject: Surgery
front: When does secondary hemorrhage occur and why?
back: After 7-14 days; due to sloughing of the vessel wall from infection.

subject: Surgery
front: What % blood volume and amount is lost in Class I hemorrhage?

subject: Surgery
front: What % blood volume and amount is lost in Class II (mild/compensated) hemorrhage?

subject: Surgery
front: What % blood volume and amount is lost in Class III (moderate/decompensated) hemorrhage?

subject: Surgery
front: What % blood volume and amount is lost in Class IV (severe) hemorrhage?
back: > 40% (> 2 litres).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the earliest sign in hemorrhagic shock?
back: Increased pulse rate (seen from Class I/II).

subject: Surgery
front: What happens to blood pressure in Class II hemorrhage?
back: Normal SBP with increased DBP (narrowed pulse pressure).

subject: Surgery
front: What happens to BP/pulse in Class IV hemorrhage?
back: Not recordable.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe mental status across hemorrhage classes II, III, IV.
back: Class II: thirsty & anxious; Class III: confused; Class IV: comatose.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the urine output in Class III vs Class IV hemorrhage?
back: Class III: decreased; Class IV: no output.

subject: Surgery
front: Give base deficit (mEq/L) for hemorrhage Classes II, III, IV.
back: Class II: -2 to -6; Class III: -6 to -10; Class IV: > -10.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Class I hemorrhage?
back: Oral liquids.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Class II hemorrhage?
back: IV crystalloids.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Class III hemorrhage?
back: IV crystalloids + colloids and blood products.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Class IV hemorrhage?
back: Massive blood transfusion.

subject: Surgery
front: After a 1-litre IV bolus, how does a 'responder' behave?
back: PR decreases, SBP increases, JVP increases; response sustained due to a one-time insult.

subject: Surgery
front: After a 1-litre IV bolus, how does a 'transient responder' behave?
back: PR increases, SBP decreases, JVP decreases; response reversed in 15-20 mins due to ongoing loss.

subject: Surgery
front: After a 1-litre IV bolus, how does a 'non-responder' behave?
back: PR increases, SBP decreases, JVP decreases; ongoing loss.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the components of damage control resuscitation?
back: Damage control surgery, permissive hypotension (SBP at lower normal limit), balanced transfusion (RBC:FFP 1:1 to prevent coagulopathy), and treating coagulopathy.

subject: Surgery
front: What does PCWP indicate, what is special about it, and how is it measured?
back: Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure indicates left heart pressure; it is the ideal indicator; measured using a Swan-Ganz catheter.

subject: Surgery
front: What does CVP/JVP indicate and what is special about it?
back: Central/jugular venous pressure indicates right heart pressure; it is the most commonly used indicator.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best clinical indicator of adequate fluid resuscitation?
back: Urine output.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the shock index formula and its significance.
back: Shock index = heart rate / systolic BP; > 0.9 indicates increased mortality rate.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the ROPE (rate over pressure evaluation) formula and its significance.
back: ROPE = pulse rate / pulse pressure; > 3 indicates decompensated hemorrhagic shock.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the modified shock index formula and its significance.
back: Modified shock index = heart rate / mean arterial pressure (MAP); it is the most sensitive index.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the end point of resuscitation?
back: Systemic perfusion.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best indicator to normalise during resuscitation?
back: Mixed venous oxygen saturation (MVOS).

subject: Surgery
front: What do base deficit and serum lactate indicate in shock?
back: Acidosis.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the definitions of massive blood transfusion.
back: Replacing the entire circulating volume in 24 hrs; > 10 units of blood in 24 hrs; or > 4 units of blood in 1 hr.

subject: Surgery
front: List the complications of massive blood transfusion.
back: Hypothermia, hypocalcemia (citrate chelates Ca2+), metabolic alkalosis > acidosis (citrate toxicity), hypomagnesemia, and hyperkalemia (RBC lysis in stored blood) > hypokalemia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of death after massive transfusion?
back: Coagulopathy.

subject: Surgery
front: How is coagulopathy prevented in massive transfusion?
back: 1:1:1 transfusion (RBC : Platelet : FFP).

subject: Surgery
front: What does TRALI stand for and what is its significance?
back: Transfusion Related Acute Lung Injury; it is the most common cause of death after transfusion.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the mechanism of TRALI?
back: Antibodies against HLA antigen -> non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.

subject: Surgery
front: Within what time does TRALI occur?
back: Within 6 hours of blood transfusion.

subject: Surgery
front: Which donor is implicated in TRALI?
back: Multiparous women.

subject: Surgery
front: What does TACO stand for and what is its mechanism?
back: Transfusion Associated Cardiac Overload; volume overload -> facial puffiness, pedal edema, breathlessness.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the chest X-ray show in TACO (per the notes)?
back: Normal.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the treatment of TACO?
back: Diuretics (Lasix).

subject: Surgery
front: Which transfusion reaction is the most severe?
back: Acute hemolytic reaction.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reactions, and how are they reduced?
back: Due to leukocytes in transfused blood; reduced with a leucoreduction filter.

subject: Surgery
front: State the perioperative RBC transfusion benefit by Hb level.
back: Hb < 6 g/dL: benefit present; Hb 6-8: benefit present with ongoing blood loss; Hb > 8: no benefit.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the hemodynamic profile of hypovolemic shock (PR, CO, SBP, PVR, extremities, JVP).
back: PR up, CO down, SBP down, PVR up, extremities cold, JVP down; corresponds to Class III hemorrhage.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the profile and cause of cardiogenic shock.
back: PR up/down, CO down, SBP down, PVR up, extremities cold, JVP up; due to pump failure (MI, heart block, arrhythmia).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the profile and cause of neurogenic shock.
back: PR down, CO down, SBP down, PVR down, extremities warm, JVP down; due to spinal cord transection (loss of sympathetic system).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the profile and cause of anaphylactic shock.
back: PR up, CO down, SBP down, PVR down, extremities warm, JVP down, MVOS up; due to increased histamine release.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the profile of warm septic shock.
back: PR up, CO up, SBP up, PVR down, extremities warm, JVP normal; a hyperdynamic state.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the profile and cause of cold septic shock.
back: PR up/down, CO down, SBP down, PVR up, extremities cold, JVP up; heart fails in sepsis due to toxins.

subject: Surgery
front: What happens to pH in shock?
back: It decreases (acidosis).

subject: Surgery
front: What is obstructive shock, its nature, and examples?
back: A type of cardiogenic shock with decreased cardiac filling; e.g. tamponade, pulmonary embolism, constrictive pericarditis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is distributive shock and which are the 'warm shocks'?
back: Blood redistributes to the peripheries; warm shocks are anaphylactic, warm septic, and neurogenic.

subject: Surgery
front: State the SIRS criteria.
back: Two or more of: temperature > 38C or < 36C; heart rate > 90/min; respiratory rate > 20/min or PaCO2 < 32 torr; WBC > 12000/mm3, < 4000/mm3, or > 10% band forms.

subject: Surgery
front: What does SIRS represent?
back: The body's response to inflammation.

subject: Surgery
front: Define sepsis.
back: SIRS + documented infection.

subject: Surgery
front: Define septic shock.
back: Sepsis + hypotension not responding to fluids.

subject: Surgery
front: Define MODS.
back: Failure of two or more organ systems.

subject: Surgery
front: State the three qSOFA criteria and the threshold for poor outcome.
back: Hypotension (systolic BP < 100 mmHg), altered mental status, and tachypnea (RR > 22/min); score of >= 2 suggests a poor outcome.

subject: Surgery
front: Within what time must the Sepsis Six be completed?
back: Within 1 hour.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the 'Give 3' of the Sepsis Six?
back: IV fluids, IV antibiotics, and oxygen.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the 'Take 3' of the Sepsis Six?
back: Blood culture, urine output, and serum lactate.

subject: Surgery
front: Within what time is the Sepsis Bundle completed?
back: Within 6 hours in a septic patient.

subject: Surgery
front: List the components of the Sepsis Bundle.
back: Maintain MAP >= 65 mmHg, maintain CVP, measure central venous oxygen saturation (ScvO2) > 70 mmHg, and remeasure lactate (maintain normal levels).

subject: Surgery
front: In breast cancer, dimpling involves which structure?
back: The ligaments of Cooper.

subject: Surgery
front: In breast cancer, nipple retraction involves which structure?
back: The lactiferous ducts.

subject: Surgery
front: Peau d'orange involves which structure, and which T-stage does skin involvement indicate?
back: Subdermal lymphatics; skin involvement indicates T4b disease.

subject: Surgery
front: What do circumferential vs slit-like nipple retraction indicate?
back: Circumferential retraction suggests malignancy; slit-like retraction suggests duct ectasia.

subject: Surgery
front: Peau d'orange over more than 1/3rd of the breast indicates which condition/stage?
back: Inflammatory breast cancer (T4d).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the three components of triple assessment of a breast lump?
back: History + clinical examination, radiological investigation, and histopathological investigation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best clinical examination method for a breast lump?
back: The dial clock method.

subject: Surgery
front: Which radiological investigation is used for breast disorders in patients < 40 years?
back: USG (to assess solid vs cystic, and in pregnancy).

subject: Surgery
front: Which radiological investigation is used for breast disorders in patients > 40 years?
back: Mammogram.

subject: Surgery
front: What is BIRADS 0 and its management?
back: Incomplete investigation; manage with additional imaging.

subject: Surgery
front: What is BIRADS 1 and its management?
back: Negative; follow up after 1 year.

subject: Surgery
front: What is BIRADS 2 and its management?
back: Benign; follow up after 1 year.

subject: Surgery
front: What is BIRADS 3 and its management?
back: Probably benign; follow up after 6 months.

subject: Surgery
front: What is BIRADS 4, its subdivisions with risks, and management?
back: Suspicious (manage with biopsy): 4a low suspicion 2-10% risk, 4b moderate 10-50% risk, 4c high 50-95% risk.

subject: Surgery
front: What is BIRADS 5 and its management?
back: Highly suggestive of malignancy; manage with biopsy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is BIRADS 6 and its management?
back: Biopsy-proven malignancy; surgical excision when appropriate.

subject: Surgery
front: Per ASBRS, what is the screening for average risk of breast cancer?
back: Annual screening mammography from 40 years of age.

subject: Surgery
front: Which categories are 'higher than average risk' for breast cancer per ASBRS?
back: BRCA mutation positive, prior chest wall radiation (10-30 years), predicted lifetime risk (by Gail model) > 20%, and strong family history.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the screening for a BRCA mutation positive patient?
back: Annual MRI from 25 years.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the screening for other higher-than-average-risk patients?
back: Annual 3D mammography/MRI from 35 years.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the radiation exposure of mammography?

subject: Surgery
front: What are the advantages of the MLO mammographic view?
back: The axilla can be visualized and maximum breast tissue is seen.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the two standard mammographic views?
back: Craniocaudal and medio-lateral oblique (MLO).

subject: Surgery
front: What does popcorn calcification on mammography indicate?
back: Fibroadenoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What does a spiculated mass on mammography indicate?
back: Malignancy.

subject: Surgery
front: List the indications for breast MRI.
back: Breast implants, multifocal & multicentric lesions, detection of local/scar recurrence, screening in young & high-risk patients, and DCIS (most sensitive).

subject: Surgery
front: Which is the imaging investigation of choice in breast disorders?
back: MRI.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the linguini sign on MRI indicate?
back: Intracapsular breast implant rupture.

subject: Surgery
front: What USG finding indicates intracapsular vs extracapsular breast implant rupture?
back: Intracapsular: stepladder sign; extracapsular: snowstorm sign.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the uses of punch biopsy in breast?
back: Skin lesions and Paget's disease of the breast.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the biopsy investigation of choice in breast, and which needle is used?
back: Tru-cut/core needle biopsy; uses a 14 G biopsy needle.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is FNAC not done for breast cancer workup?
back: It cannot differentiate between in-situ and invasive cancers and cannot identify IHC markers.

subject: Surgery
front: List the risk factors for breast cancer.
back: Increased age, early menarche/late menopause, nulliparity, smoking/alcohol, obesity, family history (maternal and paternal), HRT (estrogen + progesterone), and maternal age at first live birth > 30 years.

subject: Surgery
front: Smoking is associated with which three breast conditions?
back: Breast cancer, duct ectasia, and Mondor's disease.

subject: Surgery
front: What effect does breastfeeding have on breast cancer risk?
back: It is protective against breast cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of breast cancer?
back: Invasive ductal carcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: Which quadrant of the breast is most commonly affected, and which is least common?
back: Most common: upper outer; least common: lower inner.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common gene mutation in breast cancer, and which subtype is most aggressive?
back: BRCA 1; the most aggressive is the basal subtype.

subject: Surgery
front: Which mutation is seen in sporadic breast cancer cases?

subject: Surgery
front: On which chromosomes are BRCA 1 and BRCA 2?
back: BRCA 1 on Chr 17q; BRCA 2 on Chr 13q.

subject: Surgery
front: What percentage of breast cancers are familial, and which gene is involved?

subject: Surgery
front: Which cancers are associated more with BRCA 1 than 2?
back: Breast cancer and ovarian cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: Which cancers are associated more with BRCA 2 than 1?
back: Pancreatic cancer, prostate cancer, and male breast cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for BRCA testing in all patients?
back: Deleterious BRCA 1/2 mutation in a blood relative; and a history of ovarian, fallopian tube and/or primary peritoneal cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for BRCA testing in patients with breast cancer?
back: One or more blood relatives diagnosed with breast cancer at <= 45 years; history of bilateral breast cancer at <= 50 years; history of TNBC at <= 60 years; and history of male breast cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: What is stained in ER/PR IHC, and how is it measured?
back: The nucleus is stained (a steroid receptor); measured with the Allred score (0-8).

subject: Surgery
front: What HER2 neu IHC results are negative?
back: 0 and 1+.

subject: Surgery
front: What does a HER2 neu IHC result of 2+ mean and what is done next?
back: Equivocal; do FISH (amplified = positive, non-amplified = negative).

subject: Surgery
front: What does a HER2 neu IHC result of 3+ mean?
back: Positive.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the receptor profile of Luminal A breast cancer.
back: ER+, PR+, HER2-, Ki-67 low, CK5/6 negative.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the receptor profile(s) of Luminal B breast cancer.
back: ER+, PR+, HER2- with high Ki-67; or ER+, PR+, HER2+ with any Ki-67; CK5/6 negative.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the receptor profile of HER2-enriched breast cancer.
back: ER-, PR-, HER2+, any Ki-67, CK5/6 negative.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the receptor profile of basal-like (TNBC) breast cancer.
back: ER-, PR-, HER2-, any Ki-67, CK5/6 positive.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the receptor profile of unclassified/Claudin-low breast cancer.
back: ER-, PR-, HER2-, any Ki-67, CK5/6 negative.

subject: Surgery
front: Which molecular subtype is most common and has the best prognosis?
back: Luminal A.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe basal-like (TNBC): patient group, chemo response, prognosis.
back: Seen in young patients; best response to chemotherapy; worst prognosis.

subject: Surgery
front: How is breast tumor staging done, and what is the FDG half-life?
back: By PET-CT using 18-Fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG); FDG T1/2 is 110 minutes.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Tis stage of breast cancer?
back: Cancer in situ (DCIS, Paget's disease).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the size cut-offs for breast cancer T1, T2, T3.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines T4a breast cancer?
back: Involvement of the chest wall (serratus anterior, ribs, intercostal muscles).

subject: Surgery
front: What defines T4b breast cancer?
back: Involvement of skin (ulceration, direct infiltration, PDO, satellite nodules).

subject: Surgery
front: What is T4c breast cancer?
back: T4a + T4b.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines T4d breast cancer?
back: Inflammatory cancer: > 1/3rd breast involvement + peau d'orange.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N1 nodal stage in breast cancer?
back: Mobile ipsilateral axillary lymph node.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N2a vs N2b nodal stage?
back: N2a: fixed ipsilateral axillary LN; N2b: ipsilateral internal mammary LN.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N3a nodal stage?
back: Ipsilateral infraclavicular LN.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N3b nodal stage?
back: Ipsilateral internal mammary + axillary LN.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N3c nodal stage?
back: Ipsilateral supraclavicular LN.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common site of breast cancer metastasis, and the most common bone?
back: Bones; lumbar vertebrae are most common, due to Batson's plexus.

subject: Surgery
front: Is bony metastasis in breast cancer more osteolytic or osteoblastic?
back: Osteolytic > osteoblastic.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the current status of LCIS as an in-situ cancer?
back: It is no longer considered an in-situ cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: How do BCS and mastectomy compare in survival?
back: Overall same survival.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe BCS/lumpectomy and its mandatory follow-up.
back: Tumour removal with a 1 mm margin, followed by mandatory radiotherapy (due to increased local recurrence rate).

subject: Surgery
front: What breast volume is resected in volume-displacement vs volume-replacement oncoplasty?
back: Volume displacement: 10-15%; volume replacement: > 15% (e.g. latissimus dorsi flap).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the radiotherapy contraindications to BCS?
back: Pregnancy, prior RT to the chest wall (2 yrs), and collagen vascular disease (SLE, rheumatoid arthritis).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the technical contraindications to BCS?
back: Multicentric disease, lobular cancer (if multicentric), large tumour-to-breast ratio, locally advanced breast cancer, and multifocal disease.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures are removed in a radical/Halsted mastectomy?
back: Breast, nipple areolar complex, pectoralis major & minor, and level 1, 2, 3 axillary lymph nodes.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the modified radical mastectomy: incision and structures removed.
back: Elliptical Stewart incision; removes breast + NAC, pectoral fascia, level 1/2/3 axillary LN, and +/- pectoralis minor.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Auchincloss MRM, and is it common?
back: Pectoralis minor is retracted; it is the most common MRM.

subject: Surgery
front: What characterizes the Scanlon & Patey MRM?
back: Pectoralis minor is cut.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe a simple mastectomy and its indications.
back: Breast + NAC removed, LN not removed; done in fungating breast cancer, palliative mastectomy, DCIS, and phyllodes.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the minimum number of lymph nodes removed in axillary clearance?

subject: Surgery
front: What is the relationship of the long thoracic nerve to axillary clearance?
back: It is not a boundary and is preserved during surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: Where do level 1, 2, 3 axillary lymph nodes lie relative to pectoralis minor?
back: Level 1: lateral to; level 2: behind; level 3: medial to pectoralis minor.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the superior boundary of axillary clearance?
back: Axillary vein.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the lateral boundary of axillary clearance?
back: Thoraco-dorsal pedicle.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the medial boundary of axillary clearance?
back: Halstead ligament.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the inferior boundary of axillary clearance?
back: Angular vein.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common nerve injured in MRM, and its effect?
back: Intercostobrachial nerve; causes paraesthesia in the axilla.

subject: Surgery
front: What does long thoracic nerve (nerve of Bell) injury cause?
back: Winging of the scapula.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication of MRM, and how is it prevented and managed?
back: Seroma (fluid accumulation); prevented with a Romovac drain; managed by aspiration under aseptic conditions.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the incidence and significance of post-mastectomy lymphedema?
back: 2-15%; it is the most common cause of upper limb lymphedema, developing weeks to months post-surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What does long-standing (7-8 years) post-mastectomy lymphedema lead to?
back: Angiosarcoma (Stewart-Treves syndrome), presenting as reddish or bluish nodules.

subject: Surgery
front: What increases the incidence of post-mastectomy lymphedema?
back: Removing LN above the axillary vein and giving RT to the axilla.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for local recurrence after MRM?
back: MRI (followed by biopsy).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the TRAM flap and its drawback.
back: Transverse rectus abdominis myocutaneous flap; increased abdominal wall morbidity as muscle is removed.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the DIEP flap and why it is preferred.
back: Deep inferior epigastric artery perforator flap; the best flap, with decreased abdominal wall complications because muscle is not removed.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the sentinel lymph node, and who first described it?
back: The first draining LN in cancer; first described by Cabana.

subject: Surgery
front: In which cancers is SLNB used?
back: Malignant melanoma, breast cancer, penile cancer, vulval cancer, and head & neck cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the blue dye SLNB technique and its complications.
back: Methylene blue dye is used; complications include skin tattooing (most common), anaphylaxis, and bluish discolouration of urine.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the radionuclide SLNB technique.
back: Tc99-tagged sulphur colloid is injected, and hot (radioactive) nodes are identified on a gamma camera.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the ICG SLNB technique?
back: Indigocyanine green; a newer method.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best SLNB identification method?
back: Combination method: blue dye + radionuclide/ICG.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for chemotherapy in breast cancer?
back: LN positive, LABC, ER-/PR- tumours, and HER2 neu positive tumours.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT)?
back: LABC, TNBC, HER2 neu positive, and a large tumour in a patient preferring BCS.

subject: Surgery
front: What is RECIST complete response, and in which subtype is it maximal?
back: Disappearance of all lesions plus pathological LN; maximal in TNBC.

subject: Surgery
front: How is stage documented after neoadjuvant chemotherapy (e.g. ycT2N0M0)?
back: The prefix 'y' denotes after neoadjuvant chemo and 'c' denotes clinical staging.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a chemoport used for?
back: To deliver chemotherapy medication.

subject: Surgery
front: When can chemotherapy be avoided in early breast cancer?
back: Patient not fit/poor performance status; or early breast cancer (T1, T2/N0/M0) with hormone positive but HER2 neu negative and molecular tests showing low risk.

subject: Surgery
front: Name the molecular tests used to assess chemotherapy need.
back: Oncotype Dx (21-gene assay), Mammaprint (70-gene assay), and CANAssist.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for radiotherapy in breast cancer?
back: LN positive, tumour > 5 cm, LABC, and after BCS.

subject: Surgery
front: In which breast cancers is hormonal therapy given?
back: Only in ER+, PR+ breast cancers.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the premenopausal hormonal therapy drug, class, and duration?
back: SERM (Tamoxifen) for 10 years.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the side effects of Tamoxifen?
back: Hot flashes (most common), DVT, and endometrial hyperplasia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the postmenopausal hormonal therapy drug, class, and duration?
back: Aromatase inhibitor (Letrozole/Anastrozole) for 10 years.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the main side effect of aromatase inhibitors, and what is done prior?
back: Osteoporosis (most common); a DEXA scan is done prior.

subject: Surgery
front: Summarise the treatment indications: surgery, chemo, RT, hormonal Rx.
back: Surgery: mastectomy if BCS contraindicated; chemo: LN+, LABC, TNBC, HER2 neu+, ER+PR+ (post molecular tests); RT: post BCS; hormonal Rx: ER+, PR+.

subject: Surgery
front: Define locally advanced breast cancer (LABC).
back: T3N1M0; any T4; any N2; or any N3 (all with M0).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management sequence of LABC?
back: NACT -> MRM -> RT.

subject: Surgery
front: What is characteristic of pregnancy-associated breast cancer?
back: Aggressive tumours, usually ER, PR negative.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the diagnostic investigation in pregnancy-associated breast cancer?
back: USG, then tru-cut biopsy (diagnostic).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical approach for pregnancy-associated breast cancer?
back: BCS in the 2nd/3rd trimester only, with RT after delivery.

subject: Surgery
front: When is chemotherapy contraindicated in pregnancy-associated breast cancer?
back: In the 1st trimester.

subject: Surgery
front: When are hormonal Rx and RT contraindicated in pregnancy-associated breast cancer?
back: In all trimesters.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the incidence of male breast cancer, and how do prognosis/Mx compare with females?
back: 1% incidence; prognosis and management are the same as in females.

subject: Surgery
front: List the risk factors for male breast cancer.
back: BRCA 2 > 1, smoking/alcohol, liver disease, and Klinefelter's syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: How does DCIS typically present?
back: As pleomorphic microcalcification.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of DCIS?
back: Papillary.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most aggressive type of DCIS, and how does it present?
back: Comedo with necrosis; presents as a lump.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation for DCIS?
back: Stereotactic tru-cut biopsy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the lumpectomy-based management of DCIS?
back: BCS with mandatory RT, plus hormonal therapy if ER/PR positive; there is no role of chemotherapy.

subject: Surgery
front: When is simple mastectomy (no LN clearance) indicated in DCIS?
back: In whole-breast involvement with pleomorphic microcalcifications.

subject: Surgery
front: Which parameters are included in the Van Nuys prognostic index?
back: Size, margins, grade and necrosis, and age.

subject: Surgery
front: Which parameters are NOT included in the Van Nuys prognostic index?
back: ER and PR.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe LCIS: nature and distribution.
back: A benign disease with a risk of malignancy (not in situ); bilateral and multicentric.

subject: Surgery
front: To what can LCIS progress?
back: Invasive ductal cancer (IDC) and invasive lobular cancer (ILC).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the HPE pattern of invasive lobular cancer?
back: Indian file / single file pattern.

subject: Surgery
front: Which mutation is associated with invasive lobular cancer?
back: E-cadherin.

subject: Surgery
front: What organism causes lactational breast abscess, and from where?
back: Staphylococcus aureus, from the infant's oral cavity.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the drug of choice for breast abscess?
back: Amoxiclav/Cloxacillin.

subject: Surgery
front: How is pus in a breast abscess managed?
back: Two USG-guided aspirations; if these fail, incision and drainage.

subject: Surgery
front: In recurrent breast abscess with granulomas and positive AFB/gene expert, what is the diagnosis and treatment?
back: Tuberculosis; treated with ATT.

subject: Surgery
front: In recurrent breast abscess with granulomas but negative AFB/gene expert, what is the diagnosis and treatment?
back: Idiopathic granulomatous mastitis; treated with steroids.

subject: Surgery
front: In recurrent breast abscess, HPE showing carcinoma suggests what?
back: Inflammatory cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of a breast lump, and what is its mobility nickname?
back: Fibroadenoma; called the 'breast mouse' because it is mobile.

subject: Surgery
front: How is fibroadenoma diagnosed?
back: USG.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the mammographic finding of fibroadenoma?
back: Popcorn calcification.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the surgical options for fibroadenoma?
back: Peri-alveolar incision; or vacuum-assisted breast surgery (done more now, using a 7-10 G probe).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of a phyllodes tumour?
back: 3rd/4th decade, rapidly enlarging breast lump, and dilated veins over the chest wall.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the spread of phyllodes tumour.
back: Less than 10% metastasize to LN; if malignant, spreads to the lungs.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical management of phyllodes tumour?
back: Lumpectomy; simple mastectomy for malignant phyllodes (followed by RT) and recurrent phyllodes.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of fibrocystic disease?
back: Cyclical mastalgia plus breast nodularity.

subject: Surgery
front: How is fibrocystic disease managed?
back: Reassure it is not malignancy (2 months) and use flaxseed/evening primrose oil; if no benefit, Tamoxifen (most used) or Ormeloxifene (Centchroman).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe a simple breast cyst and its management.
back: Solid wall with no solid component (BIRADS 2); managed by observation.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe a complex breast cyst and its management.
back: Has a solid component (BIRADS 4); managed by biopsy to confirm diagnosis.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe a complicated breast cyst and its management.
back: Intracystic floating debris (infective); managed with antibiotics.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Mondor's disease, and what must always be ruled out?
back: Thrombophlebitis of the chest veins; always rule out carcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the examination finding and risk factor of Mondor's disease?
back: A cord-like swelling on examination; risk is increased in smokers.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Mondor's disease?
back: Rest (it settles spontaneously).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common pathological cause of nipple discharge, and what ducts does it involve?
back: Duct ectasia; involves multiple ducts.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the features of duct ectasia (periductal mastitis/Zuska's disease)?
back: Greenish discharge, periareolar abscess, and association with smoking.

subject: Surgery
front: How is duct ectasia managed?
back: Rule out malignancy and give antibiotics; if no response, the Hadfield procedure (excision of all ducts).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of bloody nipple discharge, and what duct does it involve?
back: Duct papilloma; involves a single duct.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the types of duct papilloma and their relative cancer risks?
back: Solitary (relative risk of Ca 1.5-2), papillomatosis (RR >= 3), and juvenile papillomatosis (young females).

subject: Surgery
front: How is duct papilloma managed?
back: Investigate with USG; surgery is microdochectomy (removal of the single duct + lump).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Paget's disease of the breast.
back: Eczema-like with destruction of the NAC; unilateral.

subject: Surgery
front: What underlying lump is found in Paget's disease and in what proportion?
back: An underlying lump in 70% of cases: DCIS (most common) or invasive ductal Ca (rare); usually ER, PR negative and CEA positive.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Paget's disease?
back: Manage the underlying lump.

subject: Surgery
front: What does punch biopsy show in Paget's disease?
back: Paget cells in the epidermis.

subject: Surgery
front: During thyroid surgery, how is the superior thyroid artery (STA) ligated and why?
back: Ligated close to the gland to save the external laryngeal nerve (ELN).

subject: Surgery
front: What muscle does the external laryngeal nerve (ELN) supply, and what is its function?
back: The cricothyroid, which is the vocal cord tensor.

subject: Surgery
front: What is special about the middle thyroid vein (MTV) in thyroid surgery, and how common is it?
back: It is the 1st vessel ligated during thyroid surgery; present in 30%.

subject: Surgery
front: Which artery helps in identifying the recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN)?
back: The inferior thyroid artery (ITA).

subject: Surgery
front: How are the capsular branches of the ITA ligated, and why?
back: Ligated close to the gland to avoid parathyroid devascularisation.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries of Beahr's triangle (used to identify the RLN)?
back: The RLN, the inferior thyroid artery (ITA), and the common carotid artery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Berry's ligament, and what is its surgical importance?
back: A pre-tracheal fascia condensation; the RLN is injured at this site during thyroid surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Pizzillo's method of thyroid examination.
back: Patient places hands on the occiput and leans back to be examined.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Lahey's method of thyroid examination used for?
back: To feel the margin of the gland.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Crile's method of thyroid examination used for?
back: To palpate nodules using the thumb.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the first investigation in thyroid disorders, and what follows it?
back: TFT (thyroid function test), followed by USG neck for a TIRADS score.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for thyroid FNAC?
back: TIRADS score TR3, TR4, TR5; and a suspicious nodule (which cannot differentiate follicular adenoma vs carcinoma).

subject: Surgery
front: What is FNAC report Thy 1 and its management?
back: Non-diagnostic; repeat FNAC under USG guidance.

subject: Surgery
front: What is FNAC report Thy 1c and its management?
back: Non-diagnostic cystic; repeat FNAC under USG guidance.

subject: Surgery
front: What is FNAC report Thy 2 and its management?
back: Non-neoplastic; follow up.

subject: Surgery
front: What is FNAC report Thy 3 and its management?
back: Follicular; hemithyroidectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is FNAC report Thy 4 and its management?
back: Suspicious of malignancy; surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is FNAC report Thy 5 and its management?
back: Malignant; surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: Which isotopes are used for a thyroid scan?
back: Technetium 99 / Iodine 123.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for a thyroid scan?
back: Features of hyperthyroidism with low TSH (FNAC is contraindicated as it triggers thyroid storm); and ectopic or aberrant thyroid tissue.

subject: Surgery
front: What does a cold nodule indicate on a thyroid scan?
back: Non-functioning nodule with increased malignancy risk (20%).

subject: Surgery
front: What does a hot nodule indicate on a thyroid scan?
back: Hyperfunctioning nodule with malignancy risk of 4%.

subject: Surgery
front: What does toxic multinodular goitre (Plummer's disease) show on scan?
back: Multiple hot and cold nodules.

subject: Surgery
front: What does Graves' disease show on a thyroid scan?
back: Diffuse increased uptake.

subject: Surgery
front: What does thyroiditis show on a thyroid scan?
back: Diffuse decreased uptake.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a thyroglossal cyst?
back: A persistent thyroglossal tract.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common location of a thyroglossal cyst?
back: Subhyoid.

subject: Surgery
front: What can a long-standing thyroglossal cyst transform into?
back: Papillary thyroid carcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of a thyroglossal cyst?
back: A midline swelling that moves on deglutition and on tongue protrusion.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of a thyroglossal cyst (Sistrunk surgery)?
back: Removal of the cyst + part of the hyoid bone + the tract up to the base of the tongue.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is incision and drainage contraindicated for a thyroglossal cyst?
back: It can cause a thyroglossal fistula.

subject: Surgery
front: What does a hemithyroidectomy consist of?
back: Lobectomy + isthmusectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the other name for near-total thyroidectomy?
back: The Hartley-Dunhill procedure.

subject: Surgery
front: What does a total thyroidectomy involve?
back: Removal of the entire gland.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is subtotal thyroidectomy not done presently?
back: It is difficult to redo surgery in case of recurrence.

subject: Surgery
front: How do hypothyroidism, RLN injury, and hypoparathyroidism rates compare among total, subtotal & near-total thyroidectomy?
back: They are equal across all three.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the Rose position for open thyroidectomy.
back: Neck extended with 30 degree head elevation.

subject: Surgery
front: Where is the collar incision placed in open thyroidectomy?
back: Two fingers' breadth above the suprasternal notch; then raise a subplatysmal flap.

subject: Surgery
front: How is the parathyroid gland identified intra-operatively?
back: As a yellowish structure.

subject: Surgery
front: Which drain is used at incision closure in thyroidectomy?
back: Romovac suction drain.

subject: Surgery
front: Which retractor is used in open thyroidectomy?
back: Joll's thyroid retractor.

subject: Surgery
front: If strap muscles must be cut, how is it done and why?
back: Cut high up, to prevent ansa cervicalis injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What does MIVAT stand for?
back: Minimally Invasive Video-Assisted Thyroid surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the approaches for MIVAT?
back: Transaxillary (most common), trans-oral, retroauricular, and nipples.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for MIVAT?
back: A nodule < 3 cm, and T1 papillary thyroid cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe external laryngeal nerve injury in thyroid surgery.
back: Most common and goes unnoticed; U/L or B/L injury causes hoarseness/inability to speak at a high pitch.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe recurrent laryngeal nerve injury in thyroid surgery.
back: Less common; U/L causes hoarseness; B/L causes stridor and aphonia (life-threatening).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of post-operative respiratory distress after thyroid surgery?
back: Laryngeal edema.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a tension hematoma after thyroid surgery managed?
back: Evacuate the hematoma to relieve pressure over the trachea.

subject: Surgery
front: List other causes of post-operative respiratory distress after thyroid surgery.
back: Laryngomalacia, bilateral RLN injury, and hypoparathyroidism (a late cause, > 48-72 hours after surgery).

subject: Surgery
front: What causes hypoparathyroidism after thyroid surgery?
back: Vascular insult to the gland (via the ITA) during surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the clinical progression of hypoparathyroidism?
back: Perioral numbness (initially) -> tingling -> tetany (Trousseau sign + and Chvostek sign +).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of death in post-op hypoparathyroidism?
back: Respiratory muscle paralysis.

subject: Surgery
front: How is post-op hypoparathyroidism with serum Ca2+ > 8 mg/dL managed?
back: Oral Ca2+ + oral vitamin D3.

subject: Surgery
front: How is post-op hypoparathyroidism with serum Ca2+ < 8 mg/dL or severe symptoms managed?
back: IV calcium gluconate + oral Ca2+ + oral vitamin D3.

subject: Surgery
front: Which syndrome and gene are associated with medullary thyroid cancer?
back: MEN 2 syndrome; RET gene.

subject: Surgery
front: Which syndromes are associated with follicular thyroid cancer?
back: Cowden syndrome and Werner syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: Which syndromes/genes are associated with papillary thyroid cancer?
back: Familial adenomatous polyposis (APC gene) and Cowden syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the age cut-off for good prognosis in differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC)?
back: Age at diagnosis < 55 years.

subject: Surgery
front: How are anaplastic cancers staged now?
back: Using the same 'T' definitions as DTC (previously classified as T4 disease).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common thyroid cancer, and its sex predilection?
back: Papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC); F > M.

subject: Surgery
front: List the risk factors for papillary thyroid carcinoma.
back: Radiation exposure to the neck (most common), long-standing thyroglossal cyst, and BRAF gene (most commonly involved).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the typical feature/pattern of papillary thyroid carcinoma?
back: Thyroid swelling that is usually multifocal.

subject: Surgery
front: How does papillary thyroid carcinoma spread?
back: Lymphatic (level 6/Delphian LN) > hematogenous (most common site: lungs).

subject: Surgery
front: What is lateral aberrant thyroid?
back: Palpable lymph node due to metastasis from papillary thyroid carcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the histology of papillary thyroid carcinoma.
back: Orphan Annie eye / coffee bean nuclei, intranuclear inclusions, and psammoma bodies.

subject: Surgery
front: Besides PTC, where else are psammoma bodies seen?
back: Serous cystadenocarcinoma of the ovary, meningioma, and papillary RCC.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for hemithyroidectomy in DTC?
back: Low-risk U/L DTC, DTC 1-4 cm, no extrathyroidal extension, radiation-induced DTC, and familial non-medullary thyroid cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for total thyroidectomy in DTC?
back: Multifocal B/L DTC, extra-thyroidal extension, and size > 4 cm.

subject: Surgery
front: When is TT + central neck dissection (CND) indicated?
back: When level 6 lymph nodes are positive.

subject: Surgery
front: When is prophylactic CND indicated?
back: In T3, T4 disease.

subject: Surgery
front: When is TT + CND + modified radical neck dissection indicated?
back: When other nodes are positive.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the post-operative management timeline for DTC.
back: Wait 4-6 weeks for TSH to rise (> 20 IU/L), do a whole-body iodine scan for residual disease/metastasis/LN+, then radioiodine ablation (I131).

subject: Surgery
front: How does I131 act, and what is its half-life?
back: Acts via beta rays; half-life is 8 days.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the lifelong follow-up for DTC?
back: Six-monthly USG neck and serum thyroglobulin.

subject: Surgery
front: What is serum thyroglobulin used for, and what level indicates recurrence?
back: Tumor marker for DTC; > 2 ng/mL indicates recurrence.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the second most common thyroid cancer, and its sex predilection?
back: Follicular thyroid carcinoma (FTC); F > M.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the risk factor for follicular thyroid carcinoma?
back: Long-standing multinodular goitre (with rapid increase in size).

subject: Surgery
front: How does follicular thyroid carcinoma spread?
back: Hematogenous to bones (most common site, may be pulsatile due to vascularity) and to lymph nodes.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the management of FTC when FNAC reports a follicular neoplasm (Thy3).
back: Hemithyroidectomy followed by frozen section; if cancer positive, manage like PTC; if negative, close up.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Hurthle cell carcinoma.
back: Earlier considered a variant of FTC; seen in the elderly (6-7th decade); more aggressive than FTC (increased risk of bone mets); less radioiodine-avid than FTC.

subject: Surgery
front: Name the prognostic indicator systems for DTC, and what MACIS represents.
back: AGES system, AMES system, and MACIS (a post-operative score including completeness of original surgical resection).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe anaplastic carcinoma of the thyroid: frequency, prognosis, age group.
back: Least common, worst prognosis, seen in the elderly.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of anaplastic thyroid carcinoma?
back: Rapidly enlarging swelling and stridor (from tracheal compression).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common site of metastasis and the genetic risk factor in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma?
back: Lungs (most common); p53 mutation.

subject: Surgery
front: How is anaplastic thyroid carcinoma managed?
back: If restricted to thyroid: aggressive surgery; if extrathyroidal spread: palliative management.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the origin of medullary thyroid carcinoma, and does it take up iodine?
back: Parafollicular 'C' cells (from ultimobranchial bodies of neural crest); they do not take up iodine.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the tumor marker for medullary thyroid carcinoma?
back: Calcitonin.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the etiology of medullary thyroid carcinoma?
back: Sporadic > familial (associated with MEN-2 syndrome).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the features of medullary thyroid carcinoma?
back: Thyroid swelling +/- diarrhea; aggressive tumors.

subject: Surgery
front: How does medullary thyroid carcinoma spread?
back: Lymphatic + hematogenous (most common: liver).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the HPE finding in medullary thyroid carcinoma?
back: Amyloid-rich stroma.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation for medullary thyroid carcinoma?
back: FNAC.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgery for MTC restricted to the thyroid?
back: TT + CND.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgery for MTC with thyroid tumor + level 6 LN?
back: TT + CND + MRND.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgery for MTC with thyroid tumor + level 6 LN + other nodes?
back: TT + CND + bilateral MRND.

subject: Surgery
front: What drugs are used for metastatic MTC?
back: Vandetanib and Cabozantinib.

subject: Surgery
front: What has no role in the management of MTC?
back: Thyroid scan and radioactive iodine ablation.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe MTC in MEN-2B syndrome.
back: The most aggressive MTC; seen in young patients; multifocal.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the mutation in MEN 1 (Wermer's syndrome)?
back: Menin gene (Chromosome 11).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the presentation of MEN 1?
back: Pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma), parathyroid adenoma/hyperplasia (most common), pancreatic endocrine tumors (gastrinomas), and others: thymic tumors, collagenomas, adrenal cortical tumors.

subject: Surgery
front: What gene and chromosome are involved in MEN 2?
back: RET gene (Chromosome 10).

subject: Surgery
front: Which exon mutation gives MTC only?
back: Exon 618.

subject: Surgery
