front: Which exon mutation is associated with MEN 2A (Sipple syndrome)?
back: Exon 634.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the presentation of MEN 2A?
back: MTC (most common), parathyroid adenoma, pheochromocytoma, and megacolon/Hirschsprung's disease.

subject: Surgery
front: Which exon mutation is associated with MEN 2B/3?
back: Exon 918.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the presentation of MEN 2B/3?
back: MTC (most common and most aggressive; prophylactic thyroidectomy by age -1 year), megacolon, medullated corneal nerve fibers, marfanoid features, and mucosal neuromas.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the mutation in MEN 4?
back: CDKN1B (Chromosome 12).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the presentation of MEN 4?
back: Pituitary adenomas, parathyroid adenomas, renal tumors, adrenocortical tumors, and reproductive organ tumors.

subject: Surgery
front: List the clinical features of hyperthyroidism.
back: Thin & irritable, weight loss despite good appetite, tachycardia, diarrhea, tremors, and heat intolerance.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism?
back: Graves' disease.

subject: Surgery
front: List the causes of hyperthyroidism.
back: Graves' disease, solitary toxic nodule, toxic nodular goitre (Plummer's disease), factitious (drug-induced), Jod-Basedow phenomenon, TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma, and struma ovarii.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Jod-Basedow phenomenon?
back: Iodine-induced hyperthyroidism.

subject: Surgery
front: What is struma ovarii?
back: Thyroid tissue in the ovary.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the management options for hyperthyroidism?
back: Drugs only (Propylthiouracil, Carbimazole); drugs followed by radioactive iodine (I131); or drugs followed by surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is PTU preferred in pregnancy, and what is its main side effect?
back: Safe in pregnancy (1st trimester); side effect is agranulocytosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What happens with inadequate preparation prior to thyroid surgery in a hyperthyroid patient?
back: Thyroid storm.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a hyperthyroid patient prepared for surgery?
back: Start anti-thyroid medications 6-8 weeks before surgery (last dose the evening before); add long-acting beta blockers (Nadolol), continued for 7 days post-surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the auto-antibodies in Graves' disease?
back: Long-acting thyroid stimulating antibodies.

subject: Surgery
front: Graves' disease is associated with which conditions?
back: Pernicious anemia and myasthenia gravis.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of Graves' disease?
back: Diffuse goitre, features of hyperthyroidism, classical eye signs, and pretibial myxedema.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Stellwag sign?
back: Infrequent blinking.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Von Graefe sign?
back: Lid lag.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Dalrymple sign and its cause?
back: Lid retraction, due to spasm of Muller's muscle.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Joffroy sign?
back: Loss of wrinkling on looking up.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Mobius sign and what does it indicate?
back: Loss of accommodation reflex; indicates severe toxicity.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the HPE findings of Graves' disease?
back: Scalloping of colloid and tall columnar cells.

subject: Surgery
front: How is Graves' disease managed in children?
back: Drugs only.

subject: Surgery
front: How is Graves' disease managed in pregnancy?
back: Drugs (1st trimester: only PTU).

subject: Surgery
front: How is Graves' disease managed in an adult without goitre?
back: Drugs followed by radio-iodine ablation.

subject: Surgery
front: How is Graves' disease managed in an adult with goitre?
back: Drugs followed by surgery (total thyroid surgery).

subject: Surgery
front: How is Graves' disease managed in the elderly with comorbidities?
back: Drugs followed by radio-iodine ablation.

subject: Surgery
front: How is Graves' disease managed in patients with eye signs, and why?
back: Drugs followed by surgery, because radio-iodine ablation worsens eye signs.

subject: Surgery
front: List the clinical features of hypothyroidism.
back: Dull, slow/lethargic, cold intolerant, bradycardia, constipation, and weight gain.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of hypothyroidism overall?
back: Iodine deficiency.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in the western world?
back: Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Wolff-Chaikoff effect?
back: Iodine-induced hypothyroidism.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Hashimoto's/lymphocytic thyroiditis: frequency, etiology, associations.
back: Most common; autoimmune; associated with Down's syndrome and Turner syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the clinical course of Hashimoto's thyroiditis?
back: Hyperthyroidism -> prolonged hypothyroidism (due to repeated attacks).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the investigations in Hashimoto's thyroiditis?
back: Autoantibodies against thyroid receptor, thyroglobulin, and thyroid peroxidase; HPE shows lymphocytic infiltration.

subject: Surgery
front: What cancers is Hashimoto's thyroiditis at risk of developing?
back: FTC and PTC.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe De Quervain's/viral granulomatous/subacute thyroiditis.
back: History of URTI; presents with a painful neck swelling.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the clinical course of De Quervain's thyroiditis?
back: Hyperthyroidism -> euthyroid -> hypothyroidism -> spontaneous recovery.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Riedel's/fibrosing thyroiditis.
back: Fibrosis within and around the gland; painless neck swelling, woody hard gland, and hoarseness of voice.

subject: Surgery
front: What investigation is done in Riedel's thyroiditis and why?
back: Biopsy, to rule out anaplastic thyroid cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a goitre?
back: Enlargement of the thyroid gland.

subject: Surgery
front: In what conditions is a diffuse goitre seen?
back: Puberty, pregnancy, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, Graves' disease, and iodine deficiency (initial phase).

subject: Surgery
front: In what condition is a multinodular goitre seen?
back: Long-standing iodine deficiency (variable gland stimulation by TSH).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe primary mediastinal (1 degree) retrosternal goitre.
back: Ectopic thyroid tissue in the mediastinum, with blood supply from mediastinal vessels.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe secondary (2 degree) retrosternal goitre.
back: Most common (90%); starts in the neck and goes behind the sternum, with blood supply from neck vessels.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of retrosternal goitre?
back: Dyspnoea, stridor, and Pemberton sign (gland compresses the thoracic inlet).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Pemberton sign?
back: Facial congestion on raising the hands above the head.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation for retrosternal goitre?
back: CECT neck/thorax.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical management of retrosternal goitre?
back: Surgery via a neck incision (90% are 2 degree); if it fails, median sternotomy.

subject: Surgery
front: List the indications for median sternotomy in goitre.
back: Primary mediastinal goitre, large malignant retrosternal goitre, recurrence in the mediastinum, and goitre size > diameter of the thoracic inlet.

subject: Surgery
front: From which pharyngeal arch does the superior parathyroid develop?
back: The 4th pharyngeal arch.

subject: Surgery
front: From which pharyngeal arch does the inferior parathyroid develop?
back: The 3rd pharyngeal arch.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the bone features of hyperparathyroidism?
back: Brown tumours (Von Recklinghausen disease of bone)/subperiosteal resorption; osteitis fibrosa cystica; and salt-and-pepper skull.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common feature ('stones') of hyperparathyroidism?
back: Multiple and recurrent renal stones.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the 'abdominal groans' of hyperparathyroidism?
back: Colicky abdominal pain and pancreatitis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the biochemistry of primary hyperparathyroidism?
back: Serum PTH up, serum Ca2+ up, serum PO4 down, urinary Ca2+ up, urinary PO4 up.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the imaging investigation for primary hyperparathyroidism?
back: Tc99 Sestamibi scan (SPECT; most sensitive).

subject: Surgery
front: How is an adenoma managed in primary hyperparathyroidism?
back: Remove the affected gland (Miami protocol).

subject: Surgery
front: How is hyperplasia managed in primary hyperparathyroidism?
back: Remove 3.5 glands; autotransplant 0.5 gland into the brachioradialis (most common) of the non-dominant forearm for easy removal if recurrence occurs.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the Miami protocol (intra-op PTH assay).
back: Compare pre-op PTH to PTH 10-15 mins after gland removal; a fall of > 50% confirms the correct gland was removed.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of secondary hyperparathyroidism?
back: Chronic renal failure (low Vit D3), lithium intake, and vitamin D3 deficiency.

subject: Surgery
front: How is secondary hyperparathyroidism managed?
back: Correction of CRF, Vit D3 supplement, low phosphate diet, and Cinacalcet.

subject: Surgery
front: What is pseudohyperparathyroidism, and which malignancy is it most associated with?
back: AKA hypercalcemia of malignancy; the most common paraneoplastic syndrome, associated with SCC lung.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the clinical feature of pseudohyperparathyroidism?
back: Altered sensorium.

subject: Surgery
front: How is pseudohyperparathyroidism managed?
back: IV fluids (1st line) followed by diuretics; drug of choice is bisphosphonates (when RFT is normal and urine output is adequate).

subject: Surgery
front: What is an adrenal incidentaloma?
back: An incidentally detected adrenal tumour.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of adrenal incidentaloma?
back: Non-functional adenoma (most common), Cushing syndrome, and metastasis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the workup for an adrenal incidentaloma?
back: Serum cortisol, plasma free metanephrines (to rule out phaeochromocytoma), serum DHEA, dexamethasone suppression test (for Cushing's), and urinary cortisol.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a functional adrenal incidentaloma without malignancy suspicion managed?
back: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a non-functional adrenal incidentaloma without malignancy suspicion managed?
back: No surgery; monitor with imaging 3-6 monthly, and operate if the size increases.

subject: Surgery
front: How is an adrenal incidentaloma with malignancy suspicion and local invasion managed?
back: Open adrenalectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: How is an adrenal incidentaloma with malignancy suspicion but no local invasion and diameter <= 6 cm managed?
back: Individualized surgical approach.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a phaeochromocytoma?
back: A tumour of the adrenal medulla.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a paraganglioma, and its most common site?
back: An extra-adrenal phaeochromocytoma; most common site is the organ of Zuckerkandl (sympathetic chain).

subject: Surgery
front: State the 'rule of 10' for phaeochromocytoma.
back: 10% familial, 10% bilateral, 10% malignant, 10% extra-adrenal.

subject: Surgery
front: What syndromes are associated with phaeochromocytoma?
back: MEN 2 (most common), neurofibromatosis-1, Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome, and familial paraganglioma syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of phaeochromocytoma?
back: Headache, sweating, and episodic hypertension (the most common sign).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the screening test for phaeochromocytoma?
back: 24-hour urine VMA (vanillyl mandelic acid).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation of choice for phaeochromocytoma?
back: Plasma free metanephrines.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the imaging investigation of choice for phaeochromocytoma?
back: MRI (light bulb sign).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for extra-adrenal phaeochromocytoma?
back: Gallium dotatate scan.

subject: Surgery
front: How is phaeochromocytoma managed?
back: Alpha blockade followed by beta blockade (Phenoxybenzamine, increasing the dose gradually until postural hypotension appears), then surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common abdominal malignancy in children?
back: Neuroblastoma > Wilms tumour.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the site of neuroblastoma?
back: Adrenal medulla > sympathetic chain.

subject: Surgery
front: What clinical features distinguish neuroblastoma from Wilms tumour?
back: Abdominal lump crossing the midline (Wilms does not cross), increased catecholamines (absent in Wilms), and metastasis present.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the metastatic features of neuroblastoma?
back: Blueberry muffin lesions on the skin and racoon eyes.

subject: Surgery
front: How is neuroblastoma managed?
back: Chemotherapy and surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the new term for carcinoids, and their most common site?
back: Neuroendocrine tumours (NET); most common site is appendix > small bowel.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe foregut/bronchial carcinoids: site, metastases, chromogranin A.
back: Site: stomach, duodenal; metastasize to bones (most common); chromogranin A positive.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the argentaffin and serotonin findings in foregut carcinoids?
back: Argentaffin negative; serotonin -/+.

subject: Surgery
front: Which carcinoid type is most common, and what is its site?
back: Midgut carcinoids; site is appendix and ileum.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe metastases, argentaffin, serotonin, and chromogranin A for midgut/hindgut carcinoids.
back: Metastasize to the liver; argentaffin positive; serotonin positive (metabolised in liver); chromogranin A negative.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the site of hindgut carcinoids?
back: Colon and rectum.

subject: Surgery
front: When does carcinoid syndrome occur in foregut vs midgut/hindgut carcinoids?
back: Foregut: if it produces serotonin; midgut/hindgut: if liver metastasis is present.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the features of carcinoids?
back: Cutaneous flushing (most common symptom), abdominal pain, and sweating.

subject: Surgery
front: What happens in carcinoid syndrome?
back: Serotonin enters the circulation, causing bronchospasm and right heart lesions.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the investigations for carcinoids?
back: Urine: 5-hydroxy indole acetic acid (5-HIAA); blood: serum chromogranin.

subject: Surgery
front: How are carcinoids managed?
back: Surgery followed by Ki67; if Ki67 is high, it is a malignant NET requiring chemotherapy.

subject: Surgery
front: How is an appendicular carcinoid < 2 cm and away from the base managed?
back: Simple appendicectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: How is an appendicular carcinoid > 2 cm and close to the base managed?
back: Right hemicolectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common site of oral cancer overall?
back: Lateral border of the tongue.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common site of oral cancer in India?
back: Gingivo-buccal sulcus.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common gene mutation in oral cancer?

subject: Surgery
front: What is the HPE of oral cancer, and its hallmark?
back: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), with keratin pearls.

subject: Surgery
front: List the risk factors for oral cancer.
back: Smoking, alcohol, betel quid, immunosuppression, sharp ill-fitting denture, and chronic infections (HPV: oropharyngeal SCC >> oral SCC).

subject: Surgery
front: Which virus is associated with nasopharyngeal cancer?
back: EBV.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe leukoplakia and its cancer risk and management.
back: A white patch that cannot be rubbed off; increases cancer risk 3-5 times; managed by biopsy (confirmatory).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe erythroplakia: appearance, cancer risk, most aggressive form, management.
back: A red patch; increases cancer risk 6-9 times; most aggressive form is speckled; managed by biopsy (confirmatory).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe chronic submucous fibrosis and its management.
back: A hypersensitivity reaction to betel nuts, causing inadequate mouth opening due to fibrous deposition; managed with intra-lesional triamcinolone.

subject: Surgery
front: How does oral candidiasis differ from leukoplakia?
back: Its white patch can be rubbed off.

subject: Surgery
front: List other conditions that increase risk for oral malignancy.
back: Chronic hyperplastic candidiasis, oral lichen planus, secondary syphilis, and Plummer-Vinson syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the other name and patient group of Plummer-Vinson syndrome?
back: Paterson-Brown-Kelly syndrome; seen in perimenopausal women.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of Plummer-Vinson syndrome?
back: Iron deficiency anemia (koilonychia +), angular stomatitis & glossitis, and post-cricoid or upper esophageal webs.

subject: Surgery
front: What cancers is Plummer-Vinson syndrome at increased risk of (due to webs)?
back: SCC esophagus and hypopharyngeal cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: What type of biopsy is taken in oral cancer, and why is the centre avoided?
back: Edge/wedge biopsy; the centre is avoided due to necrotic tissue.

subject: Surgery
front: Which pattern-of-invasion measure is a prognostic factor in oral cancer?
back: Depth of invasion (DOI).

subject: Surgery
front: What defines T1 oral cancer?
back: Size <= 2 cm and DOI <= 5 mm.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines T2 oral cancer?
back: Size <= 2 cm with DOI 5-10 mm, OR size 2-4 cm with DOI <= 10 mm.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines T3 oral cancer?
back: Size > 4 cm OR DOI > 10 mm.

subject: Surgery
front: What defines T4 oral cancer?
back: Invasion of adjacent structures.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N1 nodal stage in oral cancer?
back: Single ipsilateral LN <= 3 cm.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N2a vs N2b nodal stage in oral cancer?
back: N2a: single ipsilateral LN 3-6 cm; N2b: multiple ipsilateral LN, all <= 6 cm.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N2c nodal stage in oral cancer?
back: Any bilateral or contralateral LN, all <= 6 cm.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N3a nodal stage in oral cancer?
back: Any LN > 6 cm with ENE negative.

subject: Surgery
front: What is N3b nodal stage in oral cancer?
back: Any ENE positive (clinical/radiological), or matting/skin fixity.

subject: Surgery
front: What surgical components are used for oral cancer?
back: Wide local excision (0.5 cm margin), mandibular resection if involved, and neck dissection for LN clearance.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Commando procedure?
back: A combined procedure of tumour excision with mandibular resection and neck dissection.

subject: Surgery
front: Which incision is used for neck dissection (MRND)?
back: Modified Schoebinger's incision.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe radical neck dissection (given by Crile).
back: Removes level 1-5 LN plus 3 extra-lymphatic structures (sternocleidomastoid, internal jugular vein, spinal accessory nerve), the submandibular gland, and the tail of parotid.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe modified radical neck dissection (MRND).
back: Removes level 1-5 LN with at least 1 extra-lymphatic structure saved.

subject: Surgery
front: What is saved in MRND I, II, and III?
back: MRND I: spinal accessory nerve (SAN); MRND II: SAN + IJV; MRND III: all 3 saved (functional ND).

subject: Surgery
front: What is central neck dissection (selective ND)?
back: Removal of level 6 LN (Delphian LN).

subject: Surgery
front: What does supra-omohyoid ND (SOHND) cover, and when is it prophylactic?
back: Levels I, II and III; prophylactic in T1/T2 lesions.

subject: Surgery
front: What does extended SOHND cover?
back: Levels I, II, III and IV.

subject: Surgery
front: Which neck dissection complication has high mortality?
back: Carotid artery blowout.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common nerve injured in neck dissection, and its effect?
back: Marginal mandibular nerve / ramus mandibularis; causes drooping of the angle of the mouth.

subject: Surgery
front: How is marginal mandibular nerve injury prevented in neck dissection?
back: The incision should be 2 finger-breadths below the mandible.

subject: Surgery
front: What does spinal accessory nerve injury cause?
back: Shoulder dysfunction.

subject: Surgery
front: When is adjuvant radiotherapy indicated after oral cancer surgery?
back: With one major or two minor high-risk factors.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the MAJOR high-risk factors for adjuvant RT in oral cancer?
back: Extranodal extension (ENE/N3b) and involved margins.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the MINOR high-risk factors for adjuvant RT in oral cancer?
back: Close margins, multiple involved nodes, largest node > 3 cm, lymphovascular invasion, perineural invasion, and T3/T4.

subject: Surgery
front: Which reconstruction flap is most used by head & neck surgeons?
back: Pectoralis major myocutaneous (PMMC) flap.

subject: Surgery
front: What test is done prior to a radial artery forearm flap?
back: Modified Allen's test.

subject: Surgery
front: Which flap is used for mandibular reconstruction?
back: Free fibular flap.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of a mucus retention cyst?
back: Excision.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a ranula and its site?
back: A mucus extravasation cyst of the sublingual salivary gland; site is the floor of the mouth.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of a ranula?
back: Brilliantly transilluminant and fluctuant.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of a ranula?
back: Cyst + sublingual salivary gland excision (best treatment); or marsupialization.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the surgical complications of ranula excision?
back: Most commonly injured structure: submandibular duct; most common nerve injury: lingual nerve.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a plunging ranula and its management?
back: A mucus retention cyst of the sublingual + submandibular gland; managed by excision of the intraoral swelling + aspiration of the neck swelling.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the features of recurrent parotitis of childhood?
back: Rapid swelling of one or both glands, aggravated by chewing.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the course and age of recurrent parotitis of childhood.
back: Symptoms for 1 week followed by a quiescent period of weeks to months; age 3-6 years.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the X-ray finding and treatment of recurrent parotitis of childhood?
back: Snowstorm appearance; treated with a long course of antibiotics + endoscopic washouts.

subject: Surgery
front: Which gland is most affected by sialolithiasis, and why?
back: Submandibular gland (due to viscous and antigravity drainage) > parotid.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the composition of salivary stones, and the IOC?
back: Calcium phosphate; IOC is NCCT.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the stepwise management of sialolithiasis?
back: Endoscopic management; if it fails, duct slitting; if that fails, excision.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the frequency order of benign salivary gland tumours.
back: Parotid > submandibular > sublingual > minor.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the frequency order of malignant salivary gland tumours.
back: Minor > sublingual > submandibular > parotid.

subject: Surgery
front: Which staging system is used for salivary gland tumors?
back: The Milan system.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for salivary gland tumors?
back: FNAC.

subject: Surgery
front: What do AUS and SUMP stand for in salivary tumors?
back: AUS: atypia of undetermined significance; SUMP: salivary gland neoplasm of uncertain malignant potential.

subject: Surgery
front: What proportion of parotid tumors are benign?

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of a parotid tumor?
back: A lateral facial swelling that lifts the ear lobule; if the deep lobe enlarges, the tonsillar fossa is pushed medially.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common parotid tumor?
back: Pleomorphic adenoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common malignant parotid tumor?
back: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe pleomorphic adenoma: frequency, IOC, lobe, histology.
back: Most common, slow growing; IOC is FNAC; involves the superficial lobe; a triphasic tumor.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Warthin's tumor: frequency, laterality, sex, histology.
back: 2nd most common; most common bilateral parotid tumor; M > F; shows two layers of cells (mitochondria rich).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgery for pleomorphic adenoma and Warthin's tumor (and what is avoided)?
back: Superficial parotidectomy; incisional biopsy is avoided.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe carcinoma ex-pleomorphic adenoma and its features.
back: Malignant transformation of a pleomorphic adenoma: rapid increase in size, painless becoming painful, ulceration, facial nerve involvement, and LN involvement.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of carcinoma ex-pleomorphic adenoma?
back: Surgery followed by radiotherapy.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe adenoid cystic carcinoma of the parotid.
back: 2nd most common malignant tumor; perineural invasion causes pain and recurrence; HPE shows a Swiss cheese appearance.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical margin for parotid malignancy?

subject: Surgery
front: When is elective SOHND done in parotid malignancy?
back: For T3/T4 tumors and high-grade tumors.

subject: Surgery
front: When is adjuvant radiotherapy indicated in parotid malignancy?
back: Stage 3 and 4, high-grade tumors, positive margins, PNI+/LVI+, and ENE+.

subject: Surgery
front: Which incision is used for parotidectomy?
back: Lazy S incision / modified Blair's incision.

subject: Surgery
front: How is facial nerve injury repaired in parotidectomy?
back: Cable graft using the sural nerve.

subject: Surgery
front: What does greater auricular nerve injury cause in parotidectomy?
back: Anaesthesia over the beard region.

subject: Surgery
front: What does marginal mandibular (> cervical branch of facial nerve) injury cause?
back: Drooping of the angle of the mouth.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Frey's syndrome?
back: Gustatory sweating.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the mechanism of Frey's syndrome?
back: Auriculotemporal nerve post-ganglionic parasympathetic fibres supply skin, causing sweating over the parotid.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the investigation for Frey's syndrome?
back: Starch iodine test.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the treatment of Frey's syndrome?
back: Treatment of choice is tympanic neurectomy; also Botox.

subject: Surgery
front: How is Frey's syndrome prevented?
back: SCM flap / digastric muscle flap to cover the parotid bed.

subject: Surgery
front: On bimanual palpation, what is and isn't palpable in submandibular swellings?
back: The submandibular gland is palpable; the submandibular LN is not palpable.

subject: Surgery
front: Which nerves can be injured during submandibular gland excision?
back: Marginal mandibular nerve (most commonly injured), lingual nerve, and hypoglossal nerve.

subject: Surgery
front: Which other structures can be injured during submandibular gland excision?
back: Anterior facial vein and facial artery.

subject: Surgery
front: How does a dermoid cyst form, and what is its classical site?
back: Formed at lines of embryonic fusion; classical site is post-auricular / outer canthus of the eye.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the examination finding of a dermoid cyst?
back: A fluctuant swelling.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is imaging done before dermoid cyst surgery?
back: To rule out intracranial extension.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of a dermoid cyst?
back: Surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a tubercular cervical lymph node also called, and its significance in India?
back: A collar stud / cold abscess; the most common cause of cervical lymphadenopathy in India.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a tubercular cervical lymph node managed, and why anti-gravity aspiration?
back: Anti-gravity aspiration; dependent aspiration is avoided to prevent sinus/fistula formation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is characteristic of a cold abscess?
back: No inflammatory signs.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the 1st esophageal constriction, its distance from the incisor, and relevance?
back: Pharyngoesophageal junction (C6); 15 cm from the upper incisor; the narrowest portion of the GIT.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the 2nd esophageal constriction and its distance from the incisor?
back: Left main bronchus & arch of aorta; 25 cm from the upper incisor.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the 3rd esophageal constriction, distance, and vertebral level?
back: Where the esophagus pierces the diaphragm; 40 cm from the upper incisor; at T10.

subject: Surgery
front: How does a coin in the esophagus appear on lateral/frontal X-ray, and its symptom?
back: Lateral view: side of coin seen; frontal view: face of coin seen; symptom is difficulty swallowing.

subject: Surgery
front: How does a coin in the trachea appear on lateral/frontal X-ray, and its symptoms?
back: Lateral view: face of coin seen; frontal view: side of coin seen; symptoms are stridor & choking.

subject: Surgery
front: What X-ray sign suggests a button battery?
back: Double ring sign.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a coin foreign body beyond C6 managed vs impacted at C6?
back: Beyond C6: patient observation; impacted at C6: endoscopic removal.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a button battery in the esophagus managed and why?
back: Endoscopic removal, due to its corrosive nature (risk of perforation).

subject: Surgery
front: Which corrosive is more dangerous, and what necrosis does it cause?
back: Alkali penetrates deeper (more dangerous) and causes liquefactive necrosis.

subject: Surgery
front: Which classification grades corrosive injury?
back: Zargar classification.

subject: Surgery
front: Give Zargar grades 0 and 1.
back: Grade 0: normal; Grade 1: superficial edema/erythema.

subject: Surgery
front: Give Zargar grades 2, 3, and 4.
back: Grade 2: mucosal/submucosal ulceration; Grade 3: transmural ulceration with necrosis; Grade 4: perforation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of corrosive injury (and what is NOT done)?
back: IV fluids & NPO; NG tube should not be inserted blindly (can cause perforation); no role of prophylactic antibiotics or steroids.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most important intervention in corrosive injury?
back: Early skilled endoscopy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the definitive management of corrosive injury?
back: Management of stricture: dilatation or esophagectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is TEF Type A?
back: Isolated esophageal atresia (EA).

subject: Surgery
front: What is TEF Type B?
back: Proximal TEF with distal EA.

subject: Surgery
front: What is TEF Type C, and how common is it?
back: Proximal EA with distal TEF; the most common type.

subject: Surgery
front: What is TEF Type D?
back: Proximal and distal TEF.

subject: Surgery
front: What is TEF Type E (or H)?
back: TEF without EA (patent esophagus).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of TEF?
back: Respiratory distress, excessive drooling of saliva, coiling of the oro-gastric tube; rule out VACTERL anomalies.

subject: Surgery
front: What does VACTERL stand for?
back: Vertebral defects, Anorectal malformations, Cardiac abnormalities (most common), Tracheoesophageal atresia/fistula, REnal abnormalities, and Limb defects.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the confirmatory contrast study for TEF?
back: Iohexol > Dinosil.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for H-type TEF?
back: Combined tracheoesophagoscopy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Waterson's criteria based on?
back: Weight and the presence of pneumonia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical approach for TEF Type A?
back: If the two ends are close: anastomose; if far: gastrostomy, then anastomose when the ends are close.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical approach for TEF Types B, C, D, E?
back: Posterolateral thoracotomy and repair.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most important factor maintaining LES patency, and its normal/abnormal values?
back: Length of the intra-abdominal esophagus (3-5 cm normal; < 2 cm predisposes to GERD).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the least important factor maintaining LES patency?
back: Arrangement of mucosal folds.

subject: Surgery
front: Besides intra-abdominal length and mucosal folds, what other factors maintain LES patency?
back: Pinching effect of the right diaphragmatic crura, and orientation of the gastroesophageal angle of His.

subject: Surgery
front: What LES pressure indicates GERD?
back: LES pressure < 6 mmHg.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the predisposing factors for GERD?
back: Increased transient LES relaxation (early sign), and increased obesity with decreased H. pylori infection rate.

subject: Surgery
front: What does central obesity increase the risk of?
back: Barrett's esophagus and adenocarcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: List the clinical features of GERD.
back: Retrosternal burning (heartburn), water brash, pharyngitis/laryngitis, chronic cough, wheezing, and dental caries.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC and the gold standard investigation for GERD?
back: IOC is endoscopy; gold standard is 24-hour pH monitoring.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the medical and surgical management of GERD?
back: Medical: PPI & prokinetics; surgical: fundoplication.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for fundoplication?
back: Not responding to medical management, complications of GERD present, GERD with a large hiatal hernia, and patient wishing to stop medical management.

subject: Surgery
front: List the principles of fundoplication.
back: Restore intra-abdominal length (min 3 cm), tighten the diaphragmatic crura, wrap the fundus around the esophagus, preserve the vagus nerves, and re-establish the angle of His.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Nissen's fundoplication and its most common complication.
back: Complete 360-degree wrap (most common); most common complication is gas bloat syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: Which fundoplication is now preferred?
back: Floppy Nissen's.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the wrap angles for Dor, Toupet, and Belsey mark fundoplications.
back: Dor: 180-degree anterior; Toupet: 180-270-degree posterior; Belsey mark: 270-degree anterior.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the advantage of a partial-wrap fundoplication?
back: It prevents gas bloat.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Collis gastroplasty used for?
back: To create a neoesophagus (restores length).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the newer modalities for GERD.
back: Polymer injection (high recurrence), endoscopic RFA (good long-term results), and magnetic sphincter augmentation (LINX) in patients with minimal/no hiatal hernia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Barrett's esophagus?
back: Specialised intestinal metaplasia (squamous to columnar epithelium); red velvety mucosa; increased risk of adenocarcinoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What does endoscopic biopsy show in Barrett's esophagus?
back: Goblet cells (pathognomonic).

subject: Surgery
front: What is chromoendoscopy used for, and which dyes are used?
back: For microscopic involvement; methylene blue for Barrett's, Lugol's iodine for SCC.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the cut-offs for long-segment, short-segment, and cardia metaplasia Barrett's.
back: Long segment: > 3 cm; short segment: < 3 cm; cardia metaplasia: microscopic.

subject: Surgery
front: What do C and M represent in the Prague C & M criteria?
back: C: circumferential extent; M: maximum extent (distance in cm from the GE junction).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the Seattle protocol for biopsy in Barrett's.
back: Four-quadrant biopsies every 2 cm plus targeted biopsies of macroscopic lesions.

subject: Surgery
front: How is Barrett's with no dysplasia managed?
back: Repeat OGD every 3-5 years.

subject: Surgery
front: How is Barrett's with low-grade dysplasia managed?
back: Endoscopic ablation (RFA).

subject: Surgery
front: How is Barrett's with high-grade dysplasia managed?
back: Therapeutic intervention; 6-monthly OGD until 2 consecutive non-dysplastic biopsies.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common treatment for Barrett's, and a drawback of EMR?
back: RFA is most common; endoscopic mucosal resection has a high risk of stricture.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common esophageal cancer overall, and its location?
back: Squamous cell carcinoma; located in the middle one-third.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common esophageal cancer in the western world, and its location?
back: Adenocarcinoma; located in the lower one-third.

subject: Surgery
front: List the risk factors for SCC of the esophagus.
back: Smoking, alcohol, preservative-rich food, smoked food, tylosis, achalasia cardia, Zenker's diverticulum, corrosive injury, and Plummer-Vinson syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: List the risk factors for adenocarcinoma of the esophagus.
back: Smoking, alcohol, GERD, CREST syndrome, Barrett's esophagus, and obesity.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of esophageal cancer?
back: Progressive dysphagia (solids > liquids), weight loss, and hoarseness (a sign of advanced disease from left RLN involvement).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for esophageal cancer, for staging, and for T-staging?
back: IOC: endoscopic biopsy; staging: PET-CT (F18-FDG); T-staging: endoscopic USG.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the barium swallow appearance in esophageal cancer?
back: Rat tail appearance.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the esophagectomy margins and minimum lymph nodes removed?
back: Proximal margin 10 cm, distal margin 5 cm; minimum 15 lymph nodes.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best esophageal replacement, and on which artery is it based?
back: Gastric tube, based on the right gastroepiploic artery > right gastric artery.

subject: Surgery
front: Which esophageal replacement is used if the stomach is affected?
back: Jejunum/colon.

subject: Surgery
front: What are SEMS used for, and their most common complication?
back: Self-expanding metallic stents are used in malignant TEF; most common complication is migration.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Siewert classification used for, and what do the types denote?
back: Used for GE junction tumours; Types I & II are esophageal cancer, Type III is gastric cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe esophageal leiomyoma: frequency, sex, symptoms.
back: The most common benign tumour of the esophagus; M > F; usually asymptomatic.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the barium swallow finding of esophageal leiomyoma?
back: Punched out appearance.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of esophageal leiomyoma?
back: Enucleation using STER (submucosal tunnelling endoscopic resection).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the other name for Zenker's diverticulum?
back: Cricopharyngeal achalasia.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Killian's dehiscence?
back: A potential space between the thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus, the site of Zenker's diverticulum.

subject: Surgery
front: Is Zenker's a pulsion or traction, true or false diverticulum?
back: A pulsion diverticulum (due to increased pressure) and a false diverticulum (only mucosa comes out).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the position of Zenker's diverticulum.
back: Starts in the posterior midline, ends to the left of midline.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of Zenker's diverticulum?
back: Regurgitation (earliest), halitosis, and aspiration pneumonitis (most common complication).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for Zenker's diverticulum?
back: Barium swallow.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Zenker's diverticulum?
back: Diverticulectomy + cricopharyngeal myotomy (reduces recurrence); if unfit for surgery, endoscopic diverticulopexy using a linear stapler.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe parabronchial diverticulae.
back: True diverticulum (all layers); a traction diverticulum; caused by TB/histoplasmosis.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Type 1/sliding hiatal hernia: frequency, pathology, clinical, management.
back: The most common diaphragmatic hernia; GE junction moves proximally; GERD/asymptomatic (not life-threatening); surgery (fundoplication) only if symptomatic.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Type 2/rolling hiatal hernia: other name, pathology, risk, management.
back: AKA paraesophageal hernia; stomach portion enters the thoracic cavity with a normal GE junction; risk of volvulus & necrosis; managed with surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: What are Type III and Type IV hiatal hernias?
back: Type III: sliding + rolling; Type IV: paraesophageal with content other than stomach.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the cause and site of iatrogenic esophageal perforation?
back: Cause: post-endoscopy; site: upper 1/3rd (narrowest constriction).

subject: Surgery
front: How is iatrogenic esophageal perforation managed?
back: Small + stable + no sepsis: conservative (NPO, IV fluids, IV antibiotics, analgesics); large + sepsis: surgical repair.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Boerhaave syndrome, its cause, and patient group?
back: Spontaneous perforation; caused by forceful vomiting against a closed glottis; seen in alcoholics.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common site of Boerhaave syndrome perforation?
back: Lower 1/3rd (left posterolateral wall).

subject: Surgery
front: What is Mackler's triad in Boerhaave syndrome?
back: Subcutaneous emphysema + retching + chest pain.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Hamman's crunch?
back: A crunching sound on heart auscultation.

subject: Surgery
front: How does a Mallory-Weiss tear differ from Boerhaave (D/D)?
back: A Mallory-Weiss tear is a split in the mucosa/submucosa causing hematemesis.

subject: Surgery
front: What investigations and X-ray signs are seen in esophageal perforation?
back: Stable: CECT; unstable: contrast study; X-ray shows pneumomediastinum (Naclerio V sign, continuous hemidiaphragm, Ginkgo leaf sign).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the objectives of conservative management of esophageal perforation?
back: Seal the perforation, achieve adequate drainage, and provide nutritional support; also endoscopic sealing with clips/SEMS and T-tube placement.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a Schatzki ring, its symptom, and management?
back: A B ring (mucosal/submucosal); causes intermittent dysphagia; managed with dilatation if symptomatic.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe feline esophagus: endoscopy appearance and where it is seen.
back: Stacked-up appearance on endoscopy; seen in GERD (most common, lower 1/3rd) and eosinophilic esophagitis (upper 1/3rd).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe eosinophilic esophagitis: cause, peak age, endoscopy, biopsy criterion.
back: A chronic immune/antigen-mediated disease (food antigens); peak age 20-30 yrs; endoscopy shows rings (feline esophagus); biopsy positive at >= 15 eosinophils/hpf.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the treatment of eosinophilic esophagitis?
back: Topical steroids and PPI, with the goal of decreasing eosinophils.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe esophageal candidiasis: associations, patients, endoscopy, barium finding.
back: Associated with oral thrush; seen in immunocompromised patients; shaggy appearance on endoscopy; worm-like ulcers on barium swallow.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe CMV esophagitis: patients and ulcer type.
back: Seen in post-transplant patients/GVHD; serpiginous ulcers.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe herpes esophagitis: association and ulcer type.
back: Associated with herpes labialis; small ulcers with raised margins.

subject: Surgery
front: What is achalasia cardia and its pathophysiology?
back: The most common motility disorder; failure of the LES to relax due to loss of ganglion cells in the myenteric and Auerbach plexus.

subject: Surgery
front: What is primary vs secondary achalasia?
back: Primary: loss of ganglion cells; secondary: due to Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi).

subject: Surgery
front: What are vigorous achalasia and pseudoachalasia?
back: Vigorous: rapidly progressive; pseudoachalasia: seen in malignancy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Triple A (Allgrove) syndrome?
back: Alacrimia, Achalasia, and ACTH-resistant adrenal insufficiency.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the clinical triad of achalasia cardia?
back: Dysphagia, regurgitation (earliest), and weight loss.

subject: Surgery
front: How does dysphagia present in achalasia?
back: Initially liquids > solids.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication of achalasia?
back: Aspiration pneumonitis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the barium swallow finding of achalasia?
back: Bird's beak appearance (gradual tapering).

subject: Surgery
front: Which classification and IOC are used for achalasia?
back: Chicago classification; IOC is manometry.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Chicago Type 1 achalasia.
back: Classical; 100% failed peristalsis; DCI < 450 mmHg.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Chicago Type 2 achalasia.
back: Pan-esophageal pressurization; 100% failed peristalsis.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Chicago Type 3 achalasia.
back: Spastic; no normal peristalsis; DCI > 450 mmHg.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the components of the Eckardt score?
back: Weight loss, dysphagia, retrosternal pain, and regurgitation.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Botox for achalasia: recurrence and indication.
back: Highest recurrence; indicated for patients unfit for myotomy or pneumatic dilatation.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe pneumatic dilatation for achalasia.
back: Similar efficacy as myotomy; indicated for Type II achalasia.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Heller's myotomy: extent, complication, indication.
back: Cut 6 cm proximal to 2-3 cm distal; most common complication is GERD (prevented by fundoplication); indicated for Type I & II achalasia.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe POEM: technique and indication.
back: A longitudinal muscle cut; indicated for Type III achalasia and other spastic esophageal conditions.

subject: Surgery
front: How common is distal esophageal spasm vs achalasia, and its sex ratio?
back: 5 times less common than achalasia; F > M.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of distal esophageal spasm?
back: Angina-like chest pain and dysphagia.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the ECG and barium findings in distal esophageal spasm?
back: ECG: normal; barium: corkscrew/rosary bead appearance.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of distal esophageal spasm?
back: POEM.

subject: Surgery
front: What is CHPS, and which child does it usually affect?
back: Pyloric hypertrophy causing gastric outlet obstruction; usually affects the first-born male child.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of CHPS?
back: Normal at birth, then non-bilious vomiting at 2-3 weeks.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the examination findings in CHPS, and the best time to examine?
back: Palpable olive-shaped mass and visible peristalsis (left to right); best examined during feeding.

subject: Surgery
front: Contrast CHPS and duodenal atresia.
back: CHPS: non-bilious projectile vomiting after a few weeks, first-born male, IOC USG, treated by Ramstedt pyloromyotomy; Duodenal atresia: bilious vomiting, Down's syndrome, X-ray double bubble sign, treated by duodenoduodenostomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for CHPS and its diagnostic criteria?
back: USG; pyloric channel thickness > 4 mm and length > 16 mm.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the contrast study signs in CHPS?
back: Mushroom sign and double track/railroad sign.

subject: Surgery
front: What lab abnormality is seen in CHPS?
back: Hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria (due to repeated vomiting).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best fluid for correcting CHPS metabolic abnormality?
back: 0.45% NS + dextrose + KCl (if urine output is normal); or RL.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Ramstedt's pyloromyotomy, and when is feeding resumed?
back: Splitting of the hypertrophic muscles; feeding resumed within 4-6 hrs if uneventful, or after 24-48 hrs if there was mucosal injury.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of peptic ulcer, and its H. pylori association?
back: Duodenal ulcers (90% associated with H. pylori).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication of peptic ulcer disease, and its hemorrhage significance?
back: Bleeding; it is the most common cause of upper GI hemorrhage.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe posterior duodenal ulcers: complication, cause, management.
back: Most common complication is bleeding (due to erosion of the gastroduodenal artery); managed endoscopically (2 attempts), then open surgery if it fails.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe anterior duodenal ulcers: complication, features, X-ray, management.
back: Most common complication is perforation (perforation peritonitis); features: pain, rebound tenderness, increased HR/decreased BP, board-like rigidity; X-ray: gas under diaphragm; managed with Graham's omental patch repair.

subject: Surgery
front: What proportion of gastric ulcers are associated with H. pylori, and which classification is used?
back: 60%; the Johnson classification.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Johnson Type I gastric ulcer.
back: Along the lesser curvature; the most common type.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Johnson Type II and III gastric ulcers.
back: Type II: prepyloric + duodenal; Type III: only prepyloric; both associated with acid hypersecretion.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Johnson Type IV gastric ulcer.
back: Body of the stomach; bleeds most commonly, due to left gastric artery branches.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of gastric ulcers?
back: Biopsy must be done to rule out malignancy; antrectomy; Pauchet's procedure; Kelling-Madlener procedure.

subject: Surgery
front: Which genes are associated with H. pylori, and what does urease do?
back: CAG-A & VAC-A genes; urease helps it survive in acidic environments.

subject: Surgery
front: What conditions is H. pylori associated with?
back: Peptic ulcers, type B gastritis, gastric cancer, and MALTomas (low grade, responding to H. pylori eradication therapy).

subject: Surgery
front: Against what is H. pylori slightly protective?
back: Adenocarcinoma of the esophagus.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Bilroth 1 reconstruction.
back: Gastroduodenal anastomosis (end-to-end); high leak rate.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Bilroth 2 (Polya) reconstruction.
back: A single gastrojejunal anastomosis.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Roux-en-Y reconstruction, and its frequency.
back: Gastrojejunostomy (GJ) + jejunojejunostomy (JJ); the most common.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe truncal vagotomy: acid reduction, recurrence, complications.
back: Maximum acid reduction, least ulcer recurrence, and maximum complications.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe highly selective vagotomy: acid reduction and recurrence.
back: Least acid reduction and maximum recurrence.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common nutritional deficiency after vagotomy/reconstruction, and other deficiencies?
back: Iron deficiency (most common); also Vit B12 and Vit D3.

subject: Surgery
front: What non-nutritional complications occur after vagotomy?
back: Gall stones (truncal) and post-vagotomy diarrhoea.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Petersen's hernia, and with which reconstruction?
back: A bowel loop herniates behind the roux limb; with antecolic reconstruction.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Stemmer hernia, and with which reconstruction?
back: Bowel loops herniate through the transverse mesocolon; with retrocolic reconstruction.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe early dumping syndrome: cause, symptoms, food relation, timing.
back: Due to rapid influx of fluid into the bowel from hyperosmolar contents; epigastric fullness, nausea & vomiting; worsens with more food; starts 15-20 mins after food.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe late dumping syndrome: cause, symptoms, food relation, timing.
back: Rebound hypoglycemia from excessive insulin release; hypoglycemia (tachycardia, sweating, headache); improves with more food; starts 30-40 mins after food.

subject: Surgery
front: How is early dumping prevented?
back: Small frequent meals, avoid liquids with meals, and avoid sugar-rich liquids.

subject: Surgery
front: How is late dumping prevented?
back: Avoid simple sugars, take a high protein/fat diet, and in resistant cases try octreotide.

subject: Surgery
front: List the risk factors for gastric cancer.
back: Smoking, alcohol, preservative-rich food, H. pylori, gastric resection, polyps, gastritis, and Menetrier's disease.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the intestinal type in Lauren's classification.
back: Environmental, gastric atrophy, M>F, increases with age, gland formation, APC gene mutations.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the diffuse type in Lauren's classification.
back: Familial, blood type A, F>M, younger age, poorly differentiated, signet ring cells, decreased E-cadherin.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Japanese classification used for, and which type has the best prognosis?
back: For early gastric cancers (above the muscle layer); Type 1 has the best prognosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Borrmann's classification used for, and which type has the worst prognosis?
back: For advanced gastric cancer (invading the muscle layer); Type IV (linitis plastica) has the worst prognosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Troisier sign / Virchow LN?
back: Left supraclavicular lymph node (a sign of advanced cancer in any GI malignancy).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Irish nodule?
back: Left axillary lymphadenopathy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Blummer shelf?
back: Metastasis into the pelvis/pouch of Douglas (a sign of advanced cancer in any GI malignancy).

subject: Surgery
front: What is Sister Mary Joseph nodule, and which cancers?
back: Periumbilical metastasis; seen in gastric and ovarian cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Krukenberg tumor: laterality, cancers, HPE, spread.
back: Bilateral ovarian metastasis; seen in gastric or breast cancer; signet ring cells on HPE; spreads by retrograde lymphatic spread.

subject: Surgery
front: What are Leser-Trelat sign and tripe palms?
back: Leser-Trelat: seborrheic keratosis (internal malignancy); tripe palms: hyperkeratotic palms (internal malignancy).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for gastric cancer, for overall staging, and for T-staging?
back: IOC: endoscopic biopsy; overall staging: PET-CT; T-staging: EUS.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the primary tumor margins in gastric cancer surgery?
back: Proximal margin 5 cm; distal margin at the pylorus.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe lymph node clearance in gastric cancer (minimum, D1, D2).
back: Minimum 16 removed; D1 clearance: 1-6 LN groups; D2 clearance: 1-11 LN groups.

subject: Surgery
front: What surgery is done for Siewert type 3 gastric cancer?
back: Total gastrectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: Summarise the minimum lymph nodes removed for breast, esophagus, colorectal, and stomach cancers.
back: Breast 10, esophagus 15, colorectal 12, stomach 16.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the origin and most common site of GIST?
back: Origin: intestinal pacemaker cells of Cajal; most common site is the stomach; sporadic > familial.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Carney's triad?
back: Gastric GIST (associated with SDH-B mutation, primary imatinib resistance), pulmonary chondromas, and paragangliomas.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC and the most common clinical feature of GIST?
back: IOC: CECT (radiological diagnosis); most common clinical feature is upper GI hemorrhage.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the surgical margin for GIST, and the drug treatment?
back: 2 cm surgical margin; malignant/metastasis: surgery + Imatinib; imatinib-resistant: Sunitinib/Sorafenib.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the IHC markers of GIST?
back: CD117/CKIT (most common), CD34 (60-65%), DOG 1 (most specific); wild type is CD117 - and PDGF-alpha -.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Fletcher's classification used for, and what is it based on?
back: To differentiate benign vs malignant GIST; based on size and mitotic figures.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Borchardt's triad?
back: Unproductive retching, inability to pass a Ryle's tube, and epigastric pain.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of gastric volvulus?
back: Derotate the stomach; perform fundoplication if there is a rolling hiatal hernia.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe organoaxial gastric volvulus.
back: Most common; also has a diaphragmatic defect (rolling hiatal hernia); vascular compromise present.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe mesenteroaxial gastric volvulus.
back: Rare; diaphragmatic defects are uncommon.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a trichobezoar and its management?
back: A hair ball in the stomach causing obstruction; managed by endoscopic removal followed by psychiatric reference.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for bariatric surgery?
back: BMI > 40; BMI > 35 with obesity complications; the Asian population has a lower cut-off.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common bariatric surgery?
back: Laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most acceptable bariatric surgery?
back: Roux-en-Y gastrojejunostomy.

subject: Surgery
front: Which bariatric surgery gives maximum weight loss?
back: Duodenal switch / biliopancreatic diversion.

subject: Surgery
front: Which bariatric procedures are reversible?
back: Gastric banding and gastric balloon placement.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the risk factors in the OS-MRS (Obesity Surgery-Mortality Risk Score)?
back: Arterial hypertension, age > 45, male gender, BMI > 50, and risk for pulmonary thromboembolism.

subject: Surgery
front: Is diabetes mellitus part of the OS-MRS criteria?
back: No, diabetes mellitus is not part of the criteria.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the common complication of irreversible bariatric procedures?
back: Nutritional deficiency (iron > vit D3/Ca2+, vit B12), supplemented post-surgery.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the common channel lengths for biliopancreatic diversion, duodenal switch, and SADI-S.
back: Biliopancreatic diversion: 50 cm; duodenal switch: 100 cm; SADI-S: 250-300 cm.

subject: Surgery
front: What characterizes the duodenal switch regarding weight loss and complications?
back: Maximum weight loss (malabsorption) and maximum complications.

subject: Surgery
front: What is removed in sleeve gastrectomy, and its most common complication?
back: The greater curvature is removed; most common complication is bleeding from the staple line.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most distressing complication of sleeve gastrectomy?
back: Leak from the angle of His.

subject: Surgery
front: What happens if a sleeve redistends, and how is it managed?
back: The patient regains weight; managed with TOGA.

subject: Surgery
front: What is TOGA, and what procedure category does it belong to?
back: Trans-oral gastroplasty; a NOTES (natural orifice transluminal endoscopic surgery) procedure.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe gastric banding (band position and properties).
back: Band placed 6 cm from the GE junction; reversible and adjustable.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe intra-gastric balloon placement.
back: Endoscopic; self-dissolving after a few months.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for a mesenteric cyst?
back: CECT.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Tillaux triad of a mesenteric cyst?
back: Periumbilical swelling, Tillaux sign (swelling moves at right angle to the attachment of mesentery), and a transverse band of resonance.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the chylolymphatic mesenteric cyst.
back: Most common; sequestered lymphatic tissue; thin wall; clear fluid; independent blood supply; treated by enucleation.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the enterogenous mesenteric cyst.
back: Sequestered bowel tissue; thick wall; turbid fluid; blood supply shared with bowel; treated by resection & anastomosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of upper GI hemorrhage?
back: Non-variceal bleeding: peptic ulcer (most common), gastritis (2nd most common).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Type A gastritis: cause, associations, site.
back: Autoimmune (auto-antibodies against parietal cells), pernicious anemia, achlorhydria; site is the body of the stomach.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Type B gastritis: cause and site.
back: H. pylori induced; site is the antrum.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a Cushing's ulcer and a Curling ulcer (with sites)?
back: Cushing's ulcer: stress ulcer in head injury (site: stomach); Curling ulcer: stress ulcer in burns (site: first part of duodenum).

subject: Surgery
front: What causes AIDS-related gastritis?
back: Cryptosporidium.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe a Mallory-Weiss tear: pathology, patients, vessel, D/D, treatment.
back: Longitudinal tear in mucosa/submucosa (GE junction to cardia); most common in alcoholics after forced vomiting; vessel: left gastric artery; D/D: Boerhaave syndrome; treatment: self-limiting.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe GAVE (gastric antral vascular ectasia).
back: Autoimmune; site is the antrum; endoscopy shows watermelon stomach; treated with argon photocoagulation (APC).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Menetrier's disease.
back: Hypertrophy of gastric mucosal folds (due to TGF-alpha); protein-losing enteropathy; increased risk of cancers; treated with Cetuximab or gastrectomy (severe).

subject: Surgery
front: How is portal hypertension diagnosed?
back: Hepatic venous portal gradient (HVPG).

subject: Surgery
front: What HVPG value is normal, and what indicates preclinical sinusoidal portal HTN?
back: 1-5 mmHg is normal; 6-10 mmHg is preclinical sinusoidal portal HTN.

subject: Surgery
front: What HVPG indicates clinically significant portal HTN and risk of variceal rupture?
back: >= 10 mmHg: clinically significant (variceal formation); >= 12 mmHg: increased risk of variceal rupture.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the esophageal varices portosystemic shunt pathway.
back: Left gastric + short gastric veins to distal esophageal veins.

subject: Surgery
front: What forms caput medusae, and name other portosystemic shunt sites.
back: Periumbilical and omental veins; other sites: retroperitoneum, rectum, and bare area of the liver.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of portal hypertension?
back: Upper GI bleeding, ascites, splenomegaly, and signs of liver failure.

subject: Surgery
front: Which IV drugs are best, most used, and not used in variceal bleeding?
back: Best: IV terlipressin; most used: IV octreotide; not used: IV propranolol (lowers BP).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the endoscopic management of esophageal varices?
back: Banding (most common) > sclerotherapy (sodium tetradecyl sulphate).

subject: Surgery
front: After controlled variceal bleeding, what is the disposition?
back: 24-hour observation, then discharge on oral propranolol.

subject: Surgery
front: What is done for variceal rebleeding after endoscopy?
back: A 2nd attempt at endoscopic management; if it fails, temporary control with tubes and prepare for TIPSS.

subject: Surgery
front: When is IV pantoprazole given in variceal bleeding?
back: Post-endoscopy.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare Sengstaken Blakemore, Minnesota, and Linton tubes.
back: Sengstaken Blakemore: 3 channels, 2 balloons (most common); Minnesota: 4 channels, 2 balloons; Linton: 3 channels, 1 balloon.

subject: Surgery
front: What is TIPSS, and is it selective?
back: A shunt between the portal vein and hepatic vein; non-selective (both splenic and bowel blood are shunted).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the complications of TIPSS?
back: Rupture of capsule (earliest), blockage causing rebleeding (most common), and encephalopathy (due to the non-selective shunt).

subject: Surgery
front: Name the selective portosystemic shunts.
back: Warren (distal splenorenal shunt) and Inokuchi (left gastric venocaval shunt).

subject: Surgery
front: Name the non-selective portosystemic shunts (increased encephalopathy risk).
back: Linton (proximal splenorenal shunt) and Eck fistula (portocaval shunt).

subject: Surgery
front: Name the prognostic scoring systems for upper GI bleed.
back: Rockall's score, BLEED criteria, and Glasgow Blatchford score.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Forrest's classification used for?
back: To assess the risk of rebleeding (an endoscopic assessment).

subject: Surgery
front: Give Forrest classes Ia and Ib.
back: Ia: spurting hemorrhage (high risk); Ib: oozing hemorrhage (high risk).

subject: Surgery
front: Give Forrest classes IIa, IIb, IIc.
back: IIa: non-bleeding visible vessel (intermediate); IIb: adherent clot (intermediate); IIc: flat pigmented spot (low risk).

subject: Surgery
front: Give Forrest class III.
back: Clean ulcer base (low risk).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the cardinal features of bowel obstruction?
back: Non-passage of flatus & faeces (obstipation), vomiting (early in duodenal obstruction), distension (early in colonic obstruction), and abdominal pain.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the IOCs for bowel obstruction (initial, adults, children)?
back: Initial: X-ray abdomen erect & supine; adults: CECT; children: USG.

subject: Surgery
front: What does an erect X-ray show in bowel obstruction?
back: More than 3 air-fluid levels.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the supine X-ray features for jejunal, ileal, and large bowel obstruction?
back: Jejunum: feathery appearance, stepladder pattern (complete volvulae); ileum: featureless; large bowel: incomplete haustrations, peripheral arrangement.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the medical management of bowel obstruction?
back: NPO, IV fluids, IV antibiotics & painkillers, and Ryle's tube insertion.

subject: Surgery
front: Intra-operatively, what does a distended vs collapsed caecum indicate?
back: Distended: large bowel obstruction; collapsed: small bowel obstruction.

subject: Surgery
front: What is intussusception?
back: Telescoping of one bowel loop into another.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe primary intussusception: frequency, age, trigger, type.
back: Most common; age 6 months-2 yrs; trigger is hypertrophy of Peyer's patches; ileocolic type.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of primary intussusception?
back: Red currant jelly stools and the sign of Dance (empty RIF with a lump in the lumbar region).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe secondary intussusception: patients, cause, type.
back: Adults; secondary to a pathological lead point (polyp most common, diverticulum, cancer); colocolic type.

subject: Surgery
front: Define intussuscipiens and intussusceptum.
back: Intussuscipiens: the receiving loop; intussusceptum: the loop going inside.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the USG and contrast enema findings in intussusception?
back: USG: target/pseudokidney sign; contrast enema: pincer/claw sign.

subject: Surgery
front: What is special about contrast enema in intussusception, and its contraindications?
back: It is both diagnostic and therapeutic; contraindicated in perforation, recurrence, or a pathological lead point (surgery preferred).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the predisposing factors for sigmoid volvulus?
back: Long & narrow mesentery, redundant sigmoid, and loaded sigmoid (seen in patients on antipsychotics due to constipation).

subject: Surgery
front: In which direction does sigmoid volvulus rotate?
back: Anticlockwise > clockwise.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the X-ray sign of sigmoid volvulus?
back: Coffee bean sign (dilated large bowel).

subject: Surgery
front: Contrast sigmoid and caecal volvulus by apex and dilated bowel.
back: Sigmoid: apex points towards the right shoulder, large bowel dilated; caecal: apex towards the left shoulder, small bowel dilated.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a stable sigmoid volvulus without peritonitis managed?
back: Sigmoidoscopic decompression / flatus tube; definitive surgery is sigmoidectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the Hartmann's procedure for unstable sigmoid volvulus with peritonitis.
back: Resect the perforated segment, create a proximal colostomy, and close the distal end and keep it inside.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the contrast enema appearance in sigmoid volvulus?
back: Bird's beak appearance.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of intestinal stricture?
back: Cancer, post radiotherapy, TB, and Crohn's disease.

subject: Surgery
front: How are close vs far-apart intestinal strictures managed?
back: Close: resection & anastomosis; far apart: Heineke Mikulicz stricturoplasty.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Meckel's diverticulum: origin, border, layers, blood supply.
back: A remnant of the vitellointestinal duct; on the antimesenteric border; a true diverticulum (all layers); has an independent blood supply.

subject: Surgery
front: State the rule of 2 for Meckel's diverticulum.
back: 2% of the population, 2 inches long, 2 feet from the ileocecal junction.

subject: Surgery
front: How is an asymptomatic / broad-based Meckel's diverticulum managed?
back: No intervention.

subject: Surgery
front: How are narrow-based, diverticulitis, and perforated Meckel's managed?
back: Narrow-based: diverticulectomy; diverticulitis (mimics acute appendicitis): diverticulectomy; perforation: resection & anastomosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes bleeding in children with Meckel's, and the IOC and management?
back: Ectopic gastric mucosa > pancreatic mucosa; IOC is Tc99 pertechnetate scan; management is self-limiting, then diverticulectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: How does Meckel's present as obstruction in adults, and what is its cancer association?
back: As a pathological lead point causing intussusception; cancer is rare, most commonly a neuroendocrine tumor.

subject: Surgery
front: What does a completely patent vitelline/umbilical fistula cause?
back: Fecal discharge.

subject: Surgery
front: What does a ligament connecting the ileum to the umbilicus lead to?
back: Volvulus.

subject: Surgery
front: What do a patent umbilical end and a patent ileal end form?
back: Patent umbilical end: umbilical cyst; patent ileal end: Meckel's diverticulum.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe duodenal atresia: association, C/f, X-ray, management.
back: Common in Down's syndrome; bilious vomiting since birth; X-ray shows double bubble sign; managed by duodenoduodenostomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the X-ray sign of jejunal atresia?
back: Triple bubble sign (Stomach, Duodenum, Jejunum).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of bowel obstruction?
back: Adhesive intestinal obstruction.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of adhesive intestinal obstruction?
back: Post-surgery (most common); non-surgical causes: Crohn's disease, PID, TB, endometriosis, radiotherapy.

subject: Surgery
front: How is adhesive intestinal obstruction managed?
back: Conservative for 48-72 hours; if it fails, surgery (adhesiolysis).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the normal angle between the aorta and SMA, and what angle causes Wilkie's syndrome?
back: Normal 25-45 degrees; an angle < 22 degrees compresses D3.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes and clinical feature of SMA syndrome?
back: Causes: rapid weight loss, spinal cast; clinical feature: bilious vomiting.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for SMA syndrome?
back: CT angiography.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of SMA syndrome?
back: Encourage weight gain; Strong's procedure (duodenal derotation by cutting the ligament of Treitz); duodeno-jejunostomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the other name and etiopathogenesis of Hirschsprung's disease?
back: Congenital megacolon; absence of ganglion cells in the Auerbach (myenteric) plexus; associated with Down's syndrome and MEN 2A/2B.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of Hirschsprung's disease?
back: Non-passage of meconium (most common), distension, and constipation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for Hirschsprung's disease, and its findings?
back: Full-thickness rectal biopsy: loss of ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve trunks, and acetylcholinesterase positive on IHC.

subject: Surgery
front: What does barium enema show in Hirschsprung's disease?
back: Dilated normal proximal bowel, a transition zone, and a constricted distal part.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of Hirschsprung's disease?
back: Bypass/resection of the abnormal portion, with intraoperative frozen section.

subject: Surgery
front: What is paralytic ileus, and the most common cause of prolonged ileus?
back: A stunned bowel; hypokalemia is the most common cause of prolonged ileus.

subject: Surgery
front: Which part of the bowel is the last to recover in paralytic ileus?
back: Rectum & colon.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of acute mesenteric ischemia, and its source/risk factors?
back: Acute mesenteric artery embolism; source is the heart; risk factors are IHD and atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular pulse).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the clinical course of acute mesenteric artery embolism?
back: Sudden abdominal pain ending with peritonitis.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe acute mesenteric artery thrombosis.
back: Secondary to atherosclerosis; bowel angina (post-prandial abdominal pain, food avoidance, weight loss).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the X-ray and IOC findings in mesenteric ischemia?
back: X-ray: thumb printing; IOC: CT angiography.

subject: Surgery
front: How is early vs late mesenteric ischemia managed?
back: Early (6-8 hrs): embolectomy; late (gangrene present): bypass grafting; resection & anastomosis commonly causes short bowel syndrome.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the appendicular artery a branch of, and where is the appendicular base?
back: A branch of the lower division of the ileocolic artery; the base is at the junction of the 3 taenia coli.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common position of the appendix, and the second most common?
back: Retrocaecal (74%) is most common; pelvic (21%) is second most common.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the remaining appendix positions and which is hardest to diagnose.
back: Paracaecal 2%, subcaecal 1.5%, preileal 1%, postileal 0.5% (most difficult to diagnose).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the symptoms of appendicitis?
back: Abdominal pain (periumbilical migrating to RIF), nausea & vomiting, anorexia, and fever.

subject: Surgery
front: Where is McBurney's point?
back: At the junction of the lateral 1/3rd and medial 2/3rd of the line from the ASIS to the umbilicus.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Rovsing's sign?
back: Pain in the RIF on pressing the LIF.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the psoas sign and which appendix positions?
back: Pain in the RIF on hip flexion against resistance; in retrocaecal and pelvic positions.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the obturator sign and which position?
back: Pain on flexion + internal rotation of the hip; in the pelvic position.

subject: Surgery
front: List the components and scores of the Modified Alvarado (MANTRELS) score.
back: Migration of pain (1), Anorexia (1), Nausea/vomiting (1), Tenderness RLQ (2), Rebound pain (1), Elevated temperature (1), Leukocytosis (2), Left shift of WBC (1); total 10.

subject: Surgery
front: What Alvarado scores indicate likely and highly likely appendicitis?
back: Score > 7: likely; score > 9: highly likely.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the IOCs for appendicitis in adults and children?
back: Adults: CECT; children: USG (blind-ending tubular structure, probe tenderness, fecolith).

subject: Surgery
front: How is an inflamed appendicular base managed at appendicectomy?
back: Do not crush the base; bury it with a purse-string suture.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a gangrenous appendicular base managed?
back: Right hemicolectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: If the appendix is not inflamed at surgery, what must be ruled out?
back: Meckel's diverticulum.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the grid iron vs Rutherford Morrison incisions.
back: Grid iron (McBurney's): muscle splitting; Rutherford Morrison: muscle cutting.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the advantage of the Lanz/skin crease/bikini incision?
back: Better cosmesis.

subject: Surgery
front: Which incision is used for a perforated appendix?
back: Lower midline abdominal incision.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Gibson's incision used for, and its location?
back: Renal transplant (to identify the ureter); located above the inguinal ligament.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the complications of appendicectomy?
back: Wound infection (most common), bleeding, portal pyemia, and stump appendicitis (if the stump is > 4 mm).

subject: Surgery
front: Where do the surgeon and assistant stand in laparoscopic appendicectomy?
back: To the left of the patient.

subject: Surgery
front: In whom is appendicular perforation (omentum dysfunction) seen?
back: Children, the elderly, those with adhesions, pregnant females, and immunocompromised patients.

subject: Surgery
front: Why is appendicitis significant in pregnancy?
back: It is the most common non-obstetrical emergency; increased risk of perforation can cause preterm labor/abortions.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the clinical feature and management of appendicitis in pregnancy?
back: Pain in the RIF (can be higher up); managed by laparoscopic appendicectomy in all trimesters.

subject: Surgery
front: What regime is used for an appendicular lump?
back: The Ochsner-Sherren regime (conservative).

subject: Surgery
front: What is monitored in the Ochsner-Sherren regime, and the management?
back: Monitor size of lump, tenderness, temperature, pulse rate; manage with NPO, IV fluids, IV antibiotics, analgesics.

subject: Surgery
front: What is done if an appendicular lump recovers vs deteriorates?
back: Recovers: discharge, interval appendicectomy after 6 weeks; deteriorates: suspect abscess, extraperitoneal drainage with pigtail catheter.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common tumour of the appendix?
back: Neuroendocrine tumour (AKA carcinoid of the appendix).

subject: Surgery
front: How is an appendiceal NET close to the base and > 2 cm managed?
back: Right hemicolectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: How is an appendiceal NET close to the tip and < 2 cm managed?
back: Simple appendicectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: When is cytoreductive surgery & HIPEC done, and the HIPEC details?
back: In pseudomyxoma peritonei (mucinous peritoneal deposits); HIPEC is hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy at 41-43 degrees C using paclitaxel.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare the output of ileostomy and colostomy.
back: Ileostomy: more, liquid output; colostomy: less, semi-solid output.

subject: Surgery
