back: Features: usually asymptomatic, infertility, dilated veins (bag of worms); IOC: Doppler; treatment: percutaneous embolisation (1st line) or microsurgical varicocelectomy (most effective); recurrence is high due to dual blood supply.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Fournier's gangrene, and who is most affected?
back: Necrotising fasciitis in the perineal region (synergistic aerobic + anaerobic bacteria); most common in immunocompromised patients (DM); the testis is spared due to dual blood supply.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the C/f and management of Fournier's gangrene?
back: C/f: pain, fever, sepsis; managed with aggressive debridement (most important) and broad-spectrum antibiotics.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common testicular tumor in children, overall, and in the elderly?
back: Children: yolk sac tumor; overall: seminoma; elderly: lymphoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features of testicular tumors?
back: Painless testicular mass, abdominal lump (paraaortic LN, the 1st draining node), precocious puberty/masculinisation (Leydig cell tumor), and feminisation (Sertoli cell tumor).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the tumor markers for testicular tumors?
back: AFP, beta-hCG, LDH, and PLAP (raised in seminoma, not included in TNMS staging).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the testicular tumor pT staging.
back: pT1: involves testis and epididymis without lymphovascular invasion; pT2: with LVI; pT3: invades spermatic cord; pT4: invades scrotum.

subject: Surgery
front: How is a testicular tumor diagnosed (Chevassu manoeuvre), and what is avoided?
back: Chevassu manoeuvre: high inguinal incision and deliver the testis; if cancer, high inguinal orchidectomy; transcrotal biopsy is avoided due to upstaging risk.

subject: Surgery
front: How is stage I seminoma vs non-seminomatous tumor managed?
back: Seminoma I: one cycle of carboplatin +/- RT; non-seminomatous I: one cycle of BEP chemo + RPLND.

subject: Surgery
front: How are stage II and stage III/IV testicular tumors managed?
back: Stage II: seminoma BEP chemo, non-seminomatous BEP + RPLND; stage III/IV: seminoma BEP chemo (+ RT/RPLND if residual), non-seminomatous BEP + RPLND. (BEP = bleomycin + etoposide + cisplatin; RPLND = retroperitoneal LN dissection.)

subject: Surgery
front: What information is gathered from the driver/responder vs the patient (AMPLE)?
back: Driver/responder: mechanism of injury, injuries, signs & symptoms, treatment given; patient: Allergies, Medications, Past history, Last meal, Events leading to trauma.

subject: Surgery
front: How is the C-spine stabilised during transfer, and helmet removal?
back: 3 straps on a rigid board (head & neck, thorax, pelvis) for complete immobilization; helmet removal requires 2 persons to stabilise the C-spine.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the trimodal distribution of mortality in trauma.
back: At impact: severe head injury; within 1 hour: airway/breathing/circulatory causes; days/weeks: delayed head injury and sepsis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the golden hour?
back: Proper care in the first hour reduces mortality.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe triage priority P1 and P2.
back: P1 (Emergency/Immediate, Red): life-threatening injury, managed immediately (<= 1 hr); P2 (Urgent, Yellow): includes fractures, stabilize first as treatment can wait.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe triage priority P3, P4, and Dead.
back: P3 (Delayed, Green): walking wounded (minor bruises, lacerations), first aid; P4 (Expectant, Blue): moribund, supportive care/pain relief; Dead: Black.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the two parts of ATLS?
back: Primary survey (stabilizing life-threatening injuries: ABCD) followed by the secondary survey (detailed, to find other injuries).

subject: Surgery
front: State the NEXUS criteria, and what a positive finding warrants.
back: Neurological deficit, EtOH/intoxication, Extreme distracting injuries, Unable to provide history, Spinal tenderness (midline); if any one is positive, image the cervical spine + apply a collar.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the airway danger signs, and the failed-airway management?
back: Unable to speak, GCS <= 8, coma, severe maxillofacial injury; if airway intubation fails, emergency needle cricothyroidotomy (2-3 min procedure, 20-30 min useful), then definitive tracheostomy.

subject: Surgery
front: When is nasotracheal intubation contraindicated?
back: In suspected base of skull fracture (due to cribriform plate fracture).

subject: Surgery
front: How is breathing assessed in ATLS, and is CT part of the primary survey?
back: Chest examination, pulse oximetry (adjunct), chest X-ray (AP view), and eFAST scan; CT scan is NOT part of the primary survey.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe circulation access in ATLS.
back: Minimum 2 IV cannulas (18 G) with 1 L fluid; if IV fails: emergency intraosseous (below the tibial tuberosity) or venous cut-down (great saphenous vein, anterior to medial malleolus); definitive: central line (most common site internal jugular vein).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the CRASH-2 trial protocol.
back: If SBP < 90 mmHg or HR > 110 bpm, give tranexamic acid 1 g infusion over 10 mins followed by 1 g over 8 hrs, reducing mortality.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a pelvic binder used for?
back: Trauma with pelvic fracture; a bed sheet may be tied until a pelvic fracture is ruled out.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the GCS eye-opening responses and scores.
back: Spontaneously 4, to sound 3, to pressure 2, no response 1.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the GCS verbal responses and scores.
back: Oriented 5, confused 4, inappropriate words 3, incomprehensible sounds 2, no response 1.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the GCS best motor responses and scores.
back: Obeys commands 6, localising pain 5, normal flexion 4, abnormal flexion/decorticate 3, abnormal extension/decerebrate 2, no response 1.

subject: Surgery
front: How is GCS-P calculated?
back: GCS-P = GCS minus the Pupil Reactivity Score (both pupils unreactive = 2, one = 1, neither = 0).

subject: Surgery
front: Classify head injury by GCS.
back: Mild: 13-15; moderate: 9-12; severe: <= 8.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a log roll, and how many people are needed?
back: To examine the back of trauma patients; 4 people required.

subject: Surgery
front: What does the revised trauma score include?
back: Systolic BP, respiratory rate, and GCS.

subject: Surgery
front: What does MESS include, and its cut-offs?
back: Mangled Extremity Severity Score: type of injury, shock, signs of ischemia, age group; score <= 6 limb salvageable, >= 7 amputation necessary.

subject: Surgery
front: Which organ is most commonly injured overall/blunt, and in penetrating trauma?
back: Spleen (overall and blunt); liver (penetrating).

subject: Surgery
front: Which structures are injured in gunshot wounds, seat belt syndrome, and deceleration injury?
back: Gunshot: small intestine; seat belt syndrome: mesentery; deceleration: duodeno-jejunal flexure.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the IOCs for blunt abdominal trauma (stable vs unstable)?
back: Hemodynamically stable: CECT; unstable: FAST.

subject: Surgery
front: How is penetrating abdominal trauma managed by depth?
back: Superficial to peritoneum: local exploration & suturing; peritoneal breach (visible omentum, peritonitis, bile-stained dressing, unstable): laparotomy (remove the penetrating object in the OT due to bleeding risk).

subject: Surgery
front: What does FAST detect, and where are the probes placed?
back: Free fluid in the pericardium and abdomen; probes at epigastrium, RUQ, LUQ, and suprapubic region; eFAST adds R and L anterior thoracic.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the disadvantages of FAST?
back: Does not detect < 100 cc of fluid or hollow viscus injury, and is unreliable in penetrating trauma/retroperitoneum.

subject: Surgery
front: Which rib fractures are associated with splenic trauma?
back: Left 9-11 rib fractures.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the splenic injury Grade I and II features.
back: Grade I: subcapsular hematoma < 10% SA, parenchymal laceration < 1 cm; Grade II: SCH 10-50% SA, intraparenchymal hematoma < 5 cm, laceration 1-3 cm.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the splenic injury Grade III, IV, V features.
back: Grade III: SCH > 50% SA, IPH >= 5 cm, laceration > 3 cm; Grade IV: hilar vascular (splenic vessel) injury; Grade V: shattered spleen.

subject: Surgery
front: How is stable vs unstable splenic trauma managed?
back: Stable (IOC CECT): conservative (monitor vitals, HCT, serial 24-hr CECT), then angioembolism if grade increases, then splenic preservation surgery if it fails; Grade IV/V unstable (IOC FAST): splenectomy.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common complication of splenectomy?
back: Pulmonary (left atelectasis).

subject: Surgery
front: What hematological changes occur after splenectomy?
back: Transient rise in WBC/RBC/PLT (2 weeks); permanent: basophilic stippling, Howell-Jolly bodies, reticulocytes, hypersegmented WBCs.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe OPSI and its prevention.
back: Opportunistic post-splenectomy infection by encapsulated bacteria (pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. influenza), more in children, within the first 2 years (hematological > trauma); prevented by vaccines (elective 2 weeks prior, emergency POD 2/3).

subject: Surgery
front: How is stable vs unstable liver trauma managed, and the complications?
back: Stable: conservative; unstable: surgery; complications: hemorrhage (reactionary and secondary), biliary/vascular injury, sepsis.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Pringle's manoeuvre and its interpretation.
back: Compression of the portal triad (hepatic artery, common bile duct, portal vein) at the foramen of Winslow; if bleeding decreases, the cause is the portal vein/hepatic artery; if it continues, the cause is the hepatic veins.

subject: Surgery
front: How does packing stop bleeding in liver trauma?
back: Mops produce a tamponading effect.

subject: Surgery
front: In what is mesenteric injury most common, and how are longitudinal vs transverse tears managed?
back: Most common in seat belt syndrome; longitudinal tear (1 branch cut, no loss of vascularity): repair; transverse tear (all vessels cut, loss of vascularity): resection & anastomosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the criteria for damage control surgery (lethal triad)?
back: Hypothermia < 34 C, acidosis (pH < 7.2, lactate > 5 mmol/L), coagulopathy, BP < 70 mmHg, transfusion approaching 15 units, and injury severity score > 36.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the phases of damage control surgery (ATLS).
back: Phase 0: identification in ER; Phase 1: emergency laparotomy (stop bleeding, prevent contamination); Phase 2: ICU 24-48 hrs (correct physiology); Phase 3: re-exploration in OT (correct anatomy).

subject: Surgery
front: What is ETC (early total care)?
back: Definitive management within 36 hours of injury.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe retroperitoneal trauma Zones 1, 2, and 3.
back: Zone 1: major vessels (maximum mortality, managed surgically); Zone 2: kidney/ureter/renal vessels (IOC: stable CECT, unstable single-shot IVU); Zone 3: pelvic structures (most commonly injured, majority conservative).

subject: Surgery
front: How is most thoracic trauma managed, and the most common causes of death?
back: Most managed conservatively with chest tubes; most common cause of death in blunt trauma is tracheobronchial injury, in penetrating trauma pulmonary laceration causing haemothorax.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of thoracic trauma, and which ribs fracture in CPR?
back: Rib fractures; the 3rd-5th ribs fracture during CPR.

subject: Surgery
front: What structures are injured with 1st rib and 10th-12th rib fractures?
back: 1st rib (high impact): subclavian vessels, brachial plexus, apex of lung (rule out pneumothorax); 10th-12th ribs (floating): right = liver, left = spleen.

subject: Surgery
front: What is flail chest, and its most common cause of death?
back: Fracture of >= 2 consecutive ribs at >= 2 places; the most common cause of death is pulmonary contusion.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the clinical feature and management of flail chest?
back: Paradoxical chest wall movement (flail segment moves out on expiration, in on inspiration); managed with O2 + analgesia, then IPPV if insufficient (RR > 20/pO2 < 60), then surgical fixation.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare tension pneumothorax, hemothorax, and cardiac tamponade by pathophysiology.
back: Tension pneumothorax: penetrating/sucking wound with one-way valve, affected lung collapses with tracheal shift; hemothorax: blood in the pleural space; cardiac tamponade: penetrating >> blunt, rapid blood in the pericardial space.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare JVP, breath sounds, percussion, and cardiac sounds in the three conditions.
back: Tension pneumothorax: JVP up, absent breath sounds, hyperresonant; hemothorax: dull percussion; cardiac tamponade: JVP up, normal percussion, muffled cardiac sounds.

subject: Surgery
front: How is tension pneumothorax managed (emergency and definitive)?
back: Emergency needle thoracocentesis (adult: 5th ICS midaxillary line; children: 2nd ICS midclavicular line); definitive: tube thoracocentesis (chest tube + 3-sided occlusive dressing flutter valve).

subject: Surgery
front: How is cardiac tamponade managed?
back: Emergency thoracotomy (left anterolateral): evacuate hematoma + repair myocardium; pericardiocentesis has no role in traumatic cases.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for emergency thoracotomy?
back: > 1-1.5 litres blood at insertion, > 200 cc/hr for 3 consecutive hours, cardiac tamponade, tracheobronchial injury, and thoracic aortic injury.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the triangle of safety for chest tube insertion.
back: Apex: axilla; base: 5th intercostal space; bounded by the anterior axillary line and the mid-axillary line posteriorly.

subject: Surgery
front: On which border of the rib is a chest tube inserted, and why?
back: The upper border, never the lower (the lower border has the neurovascular bundle).

subject: Surgery
front: How is chest tube functioning assessed, and when is it removed?
back: Functioning is assessed by water column movement with every breath; removed when the lung is expanded, output < 100 cc in 24 hours, and while the patient holds their breath at peak inspiration.

subject: Surgery
front: Which side is more common in diaphragmatic injury, and the C/f and management?
back: Left > right (right protected by liver); C/f: breathlessness, bowel sounds in the thoracic cavity; managed by laparotomy (reduce bowel, repair diaphragm, ICT), avoiding blind chest tube insertion.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the three zones of neck trauma.
back: Zone 1: thoracic inlet to cricoid cartilage; Zone 2: cricoid to mandible angle (most exposed and most injured, surgically accessible); Zone 3: angle of mandible to base of skull.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the hard signs in neck trauma needing surgical exploration?
back: Subcutaneous emphysema, air bubbling from a penetrating wound, an expanding neck hematoma, and hoarseness of voice.

subject: Surgery
front: How are non-depressed and depressed skull fractures managed?
back: Non-depressed: no intervention; depressed: surgery if focal neurological signs are present or depression exceeds the depth of adjacent structures.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe an anterior cranial fossa fracture and its signs.
back: Cribriform plate fracture; black eyes/racoon eyes, CSF rhinorrhea/epistaxis (target/halo sign, B2 transferrin +), anosmia, frontal lobe contusion.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe a middle cranial fossa fracture and its signs.
back: Petrous part of temporal bone fracture; temporal bone contusions, Battle sign, hemotympanum, CSF otorrhea, facial nerve injury, paradoxical rhinorrhea.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe a posterior cranial fossa fracture and its signs.
back: Occipital bone fracture; visual problems, occipital contusion, 6th nerve injury, and Vernet/jugular foramen syndrome (9th-11th cranial nerve injury).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Battle sign?
back: Discolouration over the mastoid process.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for head injury, and the indications for CT within 1 hour?
back: NCCT; CT within 1 hour for: LOC, seizures, > 1 episode of vomiting, ENT bleed, focal neurological deficit, GCS < 13, GCS < 15 at 2 hours, open/depressed skull fracture.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for CT within 8 hours of head injury?
back: Age > 65 years and retrograde amnesia > 30 minutes.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common intracranial bleed, and how is contusion vs concussion managed?
back: Contusion is the most common intracranial bleed, managed conservatively; concussion is the mildest type, managed by avoiding contact sports for a short duration.

subject: Surgery
front: What causes secondary brain injury, and define CPP and Cushing's reflex.
back: Increased ICT; CPP = MAP - ICP (normal > 60 mmHg); trauma raises ICP then MAP, causing Cushing's reflex (decreased HR, increased BP, altered respiration).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe extradural hematoma (EDH).
back: Young patient; high-velocity impact with middle meningeal artery rupture; lucid interval; NCCT shows biconvex/lens-shaped hemorrhage; treated by craniotomy.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe chronic subdural hematoma.
back: Elderly patient; trivial injury rupturing bridging veins; not restricted by sutures; gradual altered sensorium; NCCT shows a concavo-convex shape; treated by burr hole/craniotomy.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe diffuse axonal injury (DAI).
back: Any trauma patient (most severe); high-velocity impact with shearing between grey and white matter; coma; NCCT normal, IOC is MRI (punctate hemorrhages at the grey-white junction); worst prognosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for craniotomy, and the preferred site?
back: Clot > 30 cc, midline shift > 5 mm, or clot thickness > 1.5 cm; preferred site is the pterion.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the goals in managing raised ICT?
back: CPP >= 60 mmHg, Na+ 135-145 mEq/L, SBP >= 100 mmHg, blood glucose 80-180 mg/dL; use adequate O2/perfusion, IV mannitol, hyperventilation, and no steroids (steroids are for tumours).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the Glasgow Outcome Score.
back: 1: death; 2: persistent vegetative state; 3: severe disability; 4: moderate disability; 5: good recovery.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the referral criteria to a burns unit?
back: Burns involving face/hands/feet/genitalia/perineum/major joints, chemical burns, electrical burns, inhalational injury, partial-thickness burns > 10% TBSA, and third-degree (full-thickness) burns.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the signs of airway burns warranting prophylactic intubation?
back: Burnt/singed nasal hair (most specific), hoarseness, carbonaceous sputum, closed-room burns, burns of head/face/neck, and altered sensorium.

subject: Surgery
front: When does burns inflammation cause hypovolemic shock?
back: Localised burns are < 10% TBSA; generalised burns > 10% TBSA cause hypovolemic shock (vasodilatation + leaky capillaries leading to dehydration).

subject: Surgery
front: How is fluid timed in crystalloid burns resuscitation?
back: Of the 24-hour fluid, the first half is given in the first 8 hours and the next half over the next 16 hours.

subject: Surgery
front: State the old Parkland's formula and the Galveston formula.
back: Old Parkland's: 4 x body weight (kg) x % TBSA burned; Galveston: for paediatric burns.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the Brooke's formula fluid rates for adults/older children and children <= 14 yrs.
back: Adults & older children (>= 14 yrs): 2 mL LR x kg x % TBSA (urine output 0.5 mL/kg); children (<= 14 yrs): 3 mL LR x kg x % TBSA.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the Brooke's fluid rates for infants/young children and for electrical injuries.
back: Infants & young children (<= 30 kg): 3 mL LR x kg x % TBSA + maintenance with sugar (urine output 1 mL/kg/hr); electrical injuries: 4 mL LR x kg x % TBSA until urine clears (urine output 1-1.5 mL/kg/hr).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the colloid resuscitation formula, and when is albumin given?
back: Muir and Barclay formula (most common); protein (albumin) is given after the first 12 hours to reduce edema.

subject: Surgery
front: How is TBSA calculated?
back: Wallace rule of 9 and the Lund and Browder chart (best).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe first-degree and superficial second-degree burns.
back: First degree: only epidermis, red and tender, heals without scarring in 3-5 days; superficial second degree: epidermis & papillary dermis, red/tender with blisters, heals after dressing without scarring.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe deep second-degree, third-degree, and fourth-degree burns.
back: Deep second degree: epidermis & entire dermis, red/less tender, heals with hypertrophic scars and keloids; third degree: subcutaneous tissue, black/charred/painless, early excision then STSG; fourth degree: muscle.

subject: Surgery
front: How does blanching vary across burn degrees?
back: Present in first degree and superficial second degree; only some areas in deep second degree; absent in third/fourth degree.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the key points of burns management?
back: ABCDE, wash with room-temperature water, do not burst blisters, IV fluids, avoid IM/SC injections, and insert an NG tube if > 15% TBSA (due to ileus risk).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe nutrition in burns.
back: Severe burns need 40 kcal/kg/day (twice normal); nitrogen loss is maximal on days 5-10; at least 20% of calories from proteins (Davies formula).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the purpose of escharotomy, and how is it done?
back: To prevent compartment syndrome and difficulty breathing; the deep fascia is cut and the wound extended beyond the deep burn.

subject: Surgery
front: What dressings are used for 1st and 2nd degree burns?
back: First degree: expose the wound; second degree: vaseline/paraffin gauze, collagen dressing, hydrocolloid dressing.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe silver sulphadiazine and silver nitrate as burn agents.
back: Silver sulphadiazine: the most common agent, good against pseudomonas and gram-negative bacteria; silver nitrate: good against pseudomonas but reduced action against gram-negatives.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe mafenide acetate and cerium nitrate as burn agents.
back: Mafenide acetate: painful application, penetrates eschar, causes metabolic acidosis; cerium nitrate: the best agent.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of death in burns by timing?
back: Immediate: asphyxia > neurogenic shock; early (1-3 days): hypovolemic shock; late (> 3 days) and overall: septic shock (most common organism: Pseudomonas).

subject: Surgery
front: How are acid/alkali burns managed, and which is more severe?
back: Alkali burns are more severe (liquefactive necrosis, deeper penetration); never neutralise; wash with water and brush off chemical powder.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe hydrofluoric acid burns and their management.
back: Cause increased serum K+, decreased serum Ca2+, and arrhythmias; managed with calcium gluconate.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe electrical burns and lightning injury.
back: Electrical: high-degree (3rd/4th) burns, alternating current causes arrhythmias and myoglobinuria, examine entry and exit, manage with old Parkland's formula and early excision then STSG; lightning (indirect): filigree burns (superficial burns in exposed areas).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the stages of hypothermia and the fastest rewarming method.
back: Best measured by rectal temperature; Stage I: conscious with shivering; II: impaired consciousness, no shiver; III: unconscious, no shiver, vital signs present; IV: no vital signs; cardiopulmonary bypass is the fastest rewarming method.

subject: Surgery
front: How is frostbite managed?
back: Ice crystals injure membranes and rewarming causes reperfusion injury; rewarm the leg, do not rub tissues, beware reperfusion injury (hyperkalemia and acidosis can occur), and amputate if gangrene develops.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare simple, obstructed, and strangulated hernia.
back: Simple: reducible with cough impulse and taxis possible; obstructed: irreducible, no cough impulse; strangulated: obstructed + compromised blood supply + inflamed skin, taxis contraindicated (reduction en masse risk).

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate enterocele and omentocele.
back: Enterocele: peristalsis present, difficult to reduce 1st part, tympanic; omentocele: no peristalsis, doughy, easy to reduce 1st part, dull.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the contents of Amyand and Littre hernias?
back: Amyand: appendix; Littre: Meckel's diverticulum.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe herniotomy: technique, recurrence, indications.
back: Identify the sac (defect not repaired); highest recurrence; treatment of choice in congenital inguinal hernia, inguinal hernia in children, and congenital hydrocele.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe herniorrhaphy and hernioplasty.
back: Herniorrhaphy: cut open + reduce contents, edges sutured together (used in infected and strangulated hernia); hernioplasty: close sac with mesh (least recurrence).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the best mesh material, and synthetic vs biological mesh?
back: Best: low weight (less shrinkage), thin fibres, large pores; synthetic (most common, e.g. Prolene) is avoided in infections; biological can be used in infections but is expensive.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common type of hernia, of inguinal hernia, and in females?
back: Inguinal hernia is most common overall; indirect inguinal hernia is the most common type and the most common in females (though femoral hernia is F >> M).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the single best clinical test for inguinal hernia?
back: Deep ring occlusion test (also Zieman's three-finger test and ring invagination test).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries of Hesselbach's triangle, and direct vs indirect hernia?
back: Boundaries: lateral - inferior epigastric vessels, medial - rectus abdominis, inferior - inguinal ligament; direct hernia is through the triangle, indirect is lateral to it.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries and significance of the myopectineal orifice of Fruchaud?
back: Boundaries: superior - arching fibres of internal oblique, inferior - pectineal/Cooper's ligament, lateral - tendon of iliopsoas, medial - outer border of rectus; mesh here covers inguinal, femoral, and obturator hernia defects.

subject: Surgery
front: Which nerves are injured at the superficial ring vs below the mesh in open inguinal hernia surgery?
back: At the superficial ring: ilioinguinal nerve; entrapped below mesh: iliohypogastric nerve (causing chronic pain).

subject: Surgery
front: Compare TEP and TAPP laparoscopic repairs.
back: TEP (total extraperitoneal): peritoneum remains intact; TAPP (transabdominal preperitoneal): peritoneum is breached.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries and contents of the triangle of doom?
back: Boundaries: medial - vas deferens, lateral - testicular vessels, inferior - peritoneal reflection; contents: external iliac artery, external iliac vein, genital branch of genitofemoral nerve.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the boundaries and contents of the triangle of pain?
back: Boundaries: medial - testicular vessels, lateral - peritoneal reflection, superior - iliopubic tract; contents: lateral cutaneous nerve of thigh, femoral nerve, femoral branch of genitofemoral nerve.

subject: Surgery
front: What is meralgia paresthetica?
back: Shooting pain along the lateral aspect of the thigh due to entrapment of the lateral cutaneous nerve of the thigh (most common in laparoscopy).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the corona mortis (circle of death)?
back: An abnormal communication between the obturator and iliac vessels, just behind the pubic tubercle; injury causes torrential bleeding.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe sliding and sportsman hernia.
back: Sliding (hernia en glissade): elderly males, posterior boundary formed by a visceral structure (most commonly sigmoid colon), left >> right; sportsman (Gilmore's groin): athletes, posterior wall muscle tear, sac not palpable, IOC MRI, laparoscopic repair.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the Nyhus classification of inguinal hernia.
back: Classified as primary/recurrent: lateral/indirect, medial/direct, and femoral hernia.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe femoral hernia and the femoral ring boundaries.
back: Through the femoral ring (small defect), F >> M, with high strangulation/obstruction risk (the ring cannot dilate) and possible Richter's hernia; ring boundaries: superiorly inguinal ligament, medially lacunar ligament, inferiorly pectineal/Cooper's ligament.

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate inguinal and femoral hernia on examination, and femoral hernia management.
back: Inguinal: above & medial to the pubic tubercle; femoral: below & lateral; managed by open surgery or laparoscopic hernioplasty (most common) with mesh to prevent recurrence.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common ventral/abdominal wall hernia, and the hernioplasty types?
back: Incisional hernia; hernioplasty types: onlay (over sheath), inlay (fused with sheath), intraperitoneal (IPOM, most done), retromuscular preperitoneal.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare epigastric, umbilical, and paraumbilical hernia.
back: Epigastric: xiphisternum to umbilicus, fatty hernia of linea alba, peptic-ulcer-like pain, young fit males; umbilical: through umbilicus, everted umbilicus, common in newborns (wait 2-3 yrs); paraumbilical: adjacent to umbilicus, high strangulation (narrow defect), umbilicus forms one boundary.

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate omphalocele and gastroschisis.
back: Omphalocele: defect through umbilicus, peritoneal sac present, large (liver can herniate), associated with congenital defects and Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome; gastroschisis: defect adjacent to umbilicus, no sac, bowel exposed, fewer congenital anomalies, atresia/perforation can occur.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Spigelian and obturator hernia.
back: Spigelian (intraparietal): at the outer border of rectus near the Spigelian line, between muscle layers, detected only if strangulated; obturator (little old lady's hernia): elderly multiparous women, narrow defect (Richter's risk), Howship-Romberg sign (flexion + internal rotation gives shooting pain along the obturator nerve).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Richter's and Maydl's hernia.
back: Richter's: small defect with only part of the bowel circumference herniating (seen in femoral, obturator, paraumbilical hernia; gastroenteritis is the 1st sign then strangulation); Maydl's: a W-shaped hernia with a large defect, strangulation may be missed due to the intra-abdominal connecting loop.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare Bochdalek and Morgagni congenital diaphragmatic hernias.
back: Bochdalek (most common): left posterolateral, defective pleuroperitoneal canal, herniates stomach/spleen; Morgagni: right anteromedial, defective central tendon, herniates transverse colon.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of death and ventilation rule in CDH?
back: Most common cause of death: pulmonary hypoplasia (2nd: pulmonary hypertension); IPPV is preferred and bag-and-mask ventilation is contraindicated.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Thoracoscore, and the principle of VATS?
back: Thoracoscore: a prognostic score for mortality risk in lung resection; VATS collapses the lung to create space (using a double-lumen endotracheal tube).

subject: Surgery
front: What is empyema, and how are its phases managed?
back: Pus in the pleural space; exudative phase (thick pus, fever): aspiration + antibiotics; fibrinopurulent phase: chest tube; organized phase (lung entrapped): stripping of pleura/VATS.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe SCC of the lung: sex, location, smoking, paraneoplastic, HPE.
back: M > F; central location; associated with smoking; paraneoplastic hypercalcemia; mutation p53; HPE keratin pearls (IHC: CK, p63, p40).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe adenocarcinoma of the lung.
back: F > M (most common overall); peripheral; not associated with smoking; paraneoplastic migratory thrombophlebitis; mutations Kras, EGFR, ALK; HPE glands lined by pleomorphic cells (IHC: TTF-1, Napsin A).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe small cell lung cancer.
back: M > F; central; strongest association with smoking; paraneoplastic Cushing's syndrome and SIADH; mutation L-myc; HPE salt-and-pepper chromatin, Azzopardi effect (IHC: chromogranin, synaptophysin).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe large cell lung cancer.
back: M > F; peripheral; associated with smoking; paraneoplastic gynaecomastia; HPE large pleomorphic cells.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a Pancoast tumor and its effect?
back: SCC at the apex of the lung compressing the sympathetic chain, causing Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis, enophthalmos, anhidrosis).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the lung cancer T1 sub-staging and the IOC.
back: T1a: < 1 cm; T1b: 1-2 cm; T1c: 2-3 cm; IOC is endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) FNAC.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe hamartoma of the lung.
back: The most common benign tumor of the lung; asymptomatic or with cough + hemoptysis; managed by excision with VATS.

subject: Surgery
front: List the tumors of the anterior and middle mediastinum.
back: Anterior: thymoma (most common overall), lymphoma, germ cell tumor; middle: cystic lesions, lymphoma, mesenchymal tumors.

subject: Surgery
front: List the tumors of the posterior and superior mediastinum.
back: Posterior: neurogenic tumors (most common in children), lymphoma; superior: lymphoma, thyroid, parathyroid.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe mediastinal teratoma.
back: The most common mediastinal germ cell tumor, most often in the anterior mediastinum; mature teratoma is associated with Klinefelter's syndrome; imaging shows mixed-density lesions with internal fat.

subject: Surgery
front: What is thymoma associated with, and its staging system?
back: Associated with myasthenia gravis; staged by Masaoka staging.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the Masaoka staging of thymoma.
back: I: completely encapsulated, no capsular invasion; II: invading surrounding structures + capsular invasion; III: invading neighboring organs (pericardium, great vessels, lungs); IVA: pleural/pericardial dissemination; IVB: lymphatogenous/hematogenous metastasis.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe basal cell carcinoma.
back: AKA rodent ulcer; locally invasive, does not metastasize (lymph nodes negative); most common on the face above the line joining the angle of the mouth to the ear lobule (tear-drop cancer); HPE shows a palisading pattern.

subject: Surgery
front: Which flaps are used for BCC?
back: Rhomboid flap, bilobed flap (tip of nose BCC), and bipedicled flap (eyelid region).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe superficial spreading and lentigo maligna melanoma.
back: Superficial spreading: most common overall, arises in a pre-existing mole in sun-exposed areas of young patients (e.g. subungual melanoma); lentigo maligna: in-situ melanoma in the elderly, best prognosis.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe acral and nodular melanoma.
back: Acral: most common in dark-skinned patients; nodular: most aggressive with the worst prognosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the ABCDE changes in a pre-existing mole?
back: Asymmetry, Borders (irregular), Colour change, Diameter > 6 mm, and Evolution, indicating malignant transformation.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the confirmatory Ix and staging of melanoma?
back: Biopsy with IHC markers S-100, Melan-A, HMB-45; staged by Clarke & Breslow staging (depth); prognostic factor: lymph node status > depth.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Marjolin's ulcer and its management?
back: SCC arising in a long-standing burns scar or venous ulcer; managed by excision.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common soft tissue sarcoma overall and in children?
back: Liposarcoma overall; rhabdomyosarcoma in children.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the C/f, spread, Ix, and management of soft tissue sarcoma?
back: Lump/mass; hematogenous spread; tru-cut/core biopsy; managed by wide local excision + radiotherapy (to reduce local recurrence) + chemotherapy (MAID regime); prognostic factor: tumor grade (most important).

subject: Surgery
front: Which sarcomas spread to lymph nodes, and what is a desmoid tumor?
back: Malignant fibrous histiocytoma, angiosarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, clear cell, epithelial, synovial (managed by LN clearance); desmoid tumor is a soft tissue sarcoma of the anterior abdominal wall associated with Gardner syndrome (a variant of FAP).

subject: Surgery
front: What is Virchow's triad?
back: Stasis, endothelial injury (trauma, pregnancy, malignancy), and a hypercoagulable state.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the clinical features and signs of DVT?
back: Pain & swelling, limb edema (constant sign, mostly unilateral lower limb), Homan's sign (dorsiflexion gives calf resistance), Moses sign (squeezing the calf gives pain), and chest pain (pulmonary embolism).

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate phlegmasia alba dolens and phlegmasia cerulea dolens.
back: Alba dolens: painful white/milk limb, thrombosis of major axial veins (also in pregnancy); cerulea dolens: painful blue limb, thrombosis of major axial veins + collaterals.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for DVT?
back: Doppler/duplex scan.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe anticoagulation for DVT, including in pregnancy.
back: First 5 days: LMWH + warfarin; after 5 days: warfarin only; target INR 2-3; in pregnancy: LMWH.

subject: Surgery
front: What agents are used if heparin-sensitive?
back: Fondaparinux, bivalirudin, and NOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for a Greenfield/IVC filter?
back: Recurrent thromboembolism despite anticoagulation, contraindication to anticoagulation (e.g. head injury), complications of anticoagulation, and a propagating clot despite anticoagulation.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the Well's criteria probability bands.
back: -2 to 0: low; 1 to 2: moderate; > 2: high.

subject: Surgery
front: What is post-thrombotic leg?
back: In 2/3rd of patients: secondary varicose veins, pigmentation, and lipodermatosclerosis.

subject: Surgery
front: Who is high-risk for DVT, and what prophylaxis is used?
back: Major orthopedic lower-limb surgery, major abdominal/pelvic cancer surgery, history of DVT, lower-limb paralysis or amputation; dual prophylaxis: pharmacological (LMWH, better) + mechanical (early ambulation, pneumatic compression stockings).

subject: Surgery
front: Which nerves accompany the GSV and SSV, and what is the vein of Giacomini?
back: GSV: saphenous nerve (below knee); SSV: sural nerve; the vein of Giacomini connects SSV to GSV and increases recurrence.

subject: Surgery
front: Name the perforators and their locations.
back: Hunterian (thigh), Dodd (above knee), Boyd (below knee), Cockett (5, 10, 15 cm above the medial malleolus), and May/Kuster (heel).

subject: Surgery
front: Classify dilated veins by size.
back: > 3 mm: varicose veins; 1-3 mm: reticular veins; < 1 mm: thread veins/dermal flares/telangiectatic veins.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe corona phlebectasiae, atrophie blanche, and lipodermatosclerosis.
back: Corona phlebectasiae/malleolar flare: fan-shaped collection of thread veins (early sign of advanced venous disease); atrophie blanche: areas of hypopigmentation; lipodermatosclerosis: skin thinning, tendoachilles contracture, inverted champagne bottle appearance.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the CEAP classification C0 to C3.
back: C0: no signs; C1: telangiectasias/reticular veins; C2: varicose veins; C3: edema.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the CEAP classification C4 to C6.
back: C4: skin/subcutaneous changes (C4a pigmentation/eczema, C4b lipodermatosclerosis/atrophie blanche, C4c corona phlebectatica); C5: healed ulcer; C6: active venous ulcer.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for varicose veins, and the Mickey mouse sign?
back: Duplex scan; Mickey mouse sign is CFA + GSV + femoral vein (profunda femoris is not part of it).

subject: Surgery
front: Name the clinical tests for SFJ incompetence, perforator incompetence, and DVT.
back: SFJ incompetence: Trendelenburg test, Morrisey cough impulse, Schwartz test; perforator incompetence: Trendelenburg test, multiple tourniquet test, Fegan's method; DVT: modified Perthe's test.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the traditional surgery for SFJ/GSV vs SPJ/SSV incompetence?
back: SFJ/GSV: Trendelenburg surgery (flush ligation of SFJ + tributary ligation) + stripping of the vein till the knee; SPJ/SSV: flush ligation of SPJ (no stripping, to prevent sural nerve injury).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the latest surgeries for varicose veins and perforator incompetence?
back: Latest: IVLT (intravenous laser therapy) and RFA (radiofrequency ablation); perforator incompetence: Dodd & Cockett procedure and SEPS (subfascial endoscopic perforator surgery).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe foam sclerotherapy and the most common surgical complication.
back: Sclerosant is sodium tetradecyl sulphate with air:sclerosant 3:1 or 4:1, used for thread veins/dermal flares; the most common complication of varicose vein surgery is nerve injury (recurrence is SSV > GSV).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe venous ulcer: site, features, and management.
back: Most common site is the medial malleolus/Gaiter's area; shallow ulcer with sloping edges, pale granulation tissue, pigmented margins; managed by Bisgaard's regime (education, elevation, elastic compression stockings grade III, dressings), surgery, and pentoxifylline (the only approved drug).

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate venous, arterial, trophic, and diabetic ulcers by site and margins.
back: Venous: medial malleolus/Gaiter, sloping margins; arterial: dorsum/lateral side, absent pulses, punched-out; trophic: sole/base of great toe, reduced sensation, punched-out; diabetic: sole/base of great toe, punched-out.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cause of acute arterial occlusion, and the 6 Ps?
back: Embolus (most common source: heart, with IHD/atrial fibrillation); the 6 Ps: Pain, Pallor, Paresthesia, Poikilothermia (cold limbs), Pulselessness, and Paresis/paralysis (late).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the management of acute arterial occlusion?
back: Embolectomy using a Fogarty's balloon.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe chronic arterial occlusion and its clinical features.
back: Gradual occlusion by thrombus leading to collateral formation; features: intermittent claudication (cramping pain), rest pain (progressive), and gangrene.

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate vascular and neurogenic claudication.
back: Vascular: cramping pain after walking a set distance, one muscle level below the block, progresses to rest pain; neurogenic: pain varies with posture (relieved bending forward, aggravated standing straight) due to lumbar canal stenosis.

subject: Surgery
front: Give Boyd's classification of claudication.
back: Class 1: pain on walking that reduces as walking continues; Class 2: able to walk despite pain; Class 3: pain forces the patient to stop; Class 4: pain at rest.

subject: Surgery
front: What is aortoiliac obstruction (Leriche syndrome)?
back: A block at the aortic bifurcation causing claudication in the buttock (earliest) and thigh, and impotence in males.

subject: Surgery
front: How is the ABPI calculated and interpreted?
back: ABPI = max systolic BP at ankle / max systolic BP at brachial artery; > 1.4: calcified vessels (DM/CKD); 0.9-1.4: normal; < 0.9: claudication; < 0.4: chronic limb-threatening ischemia.

subject: Surgery
front: What does a 20% ABPI drop after exercise and TBI indicate?
back: A > 20% drop indicates flow-limiting arterial disease; a 0.1 fall in ABPI raises cardiac mortality risk by 10%; TBI is more reliable in DM (where ABPI is falsely raised), and TBI < 0.6 indicates an arterial lesion.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare Buerger's disease and atherosclerosis.
back: Buerger's (thromboangiitis obliterans): M > F, 3rd decade, smoking, affects artery/vein/nerve, distal to proximal, small-medium vessels, corkscrew collaterals on CT; atherosclerosis: M = F, >= 5th decade, multiple risk factors, affects arteries, proximal to distal, large-medium vessels.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the treatment of Buerger's disease?
back: Stop smoking, pentoxyphylline, conservative amputation, and lumbar sympathectomy (only if rest pain present; if bilateral, conserve the L1 ganglion on one side to prevent impotence).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe atherosclerosis treatment and the grafts used.
back: Angioplasty (first line; higher success iliac & femoropopliteal, lower below knee) and grafting: Leriche's aortobifemoral (Dacron); infrainguinal natural reverse saphenous vein (best) or synthetic PTFE.

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate dry and wet gangrene.
back: Dry: gradual slowing of blood, good line of demarcation; wet: venous blockade or superadded infection, poor line of demarcation (can extend proximally with infection).

subject: Surgery
front: Name the most common aneurysm vessels (overall, extracranial, peripheral, visceral, mycotic).
back: Overall: circle of Willis; extracranial: infrarenal abdominal aorta; peripheral: popliteal artery; visceral: splenic artery; mycotic: aorta (due to S. aureus).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe AAA: site, risk factor, screening.
back: Most common site is the infrarenal abdominal aorta; the most important risk factor is atherosclerosis; screening is USG from 65 years.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the critical diameters for aneurysm rupture risk.
back: AAA and ascending thoracic aneurysm: 5.5 cm; descending thoracic aneurysm: 6 cm (rupture risk rises beyond these).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the C/f and IOC of AAA?
back: Asymptomatic, blue toe syndrome (emboli), rupture into the left retroperitoneum (most common), abdominal pain, and a mass; IOC is CT angiography.

subject: Surgery
front: How is AAA treated (>= 5.5 cm or symptomatic)?
back: Open surgical repair (Mattox maneuver, Dacron graft) or EVAR (endovascular aneurysm repair).

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate the Mattox and Cattle-Brasch manoeuvres.
back: Mattox: left medial visceral rotation of the descending colon, exposing the abdominal aorta; Cattle-Brasch: right medial visceral rotation of the ascending colon, exposing the IVC.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Type 1 and Type 2 endoleaks after EVAR.
back: Type 1: improper seal, most common in thoracic aortic aneurysm repair; Type 2: retrograde leak from lumbar vessels, most common in abdominal aortic aneurysm repair.

subject: Surgery
front: List the complications of AAA surgery.
back: Cardiovascular complications, renal failure, aortoduodenal fistula (haematemesis), left-sided colonic ischemia (splenic flexure watershed, bloody diarrhea), paraparesis (artery of Adamkiewicz); mortality 2-3% (> 50% if ruptured).

subject: Surgery
front: What is aortic dissection, and its most common site and trigger?
back: A false lumen from an intimal tear with blood flowing between the intima and media; most common site is the lateral wall of the ascending thoracic aorta; most common trigger is HTN.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common clinical feature and the investigations of aortic dissection?
back: Chest pain radiating to the back (interscapular, proceeding downwards); investigations: chest X-ray (widened mediastinum), CT angiography (IOC if stable), transesophageal echocardiography (IOC if unstable).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the DeBakey and Stanford classification of aortic dissection.
back: DeBakey I (most common): ascending + descending (Stanford A); II: only ascending (Stanford A); III: only descending (Stanford B).

subject: Surgery
front: How is aortic dissection managed?
back: Short-acting beta-blocker (esmolol) for permissive hypotension; DeBakey 1 & 2: graft repair (open/EVAR); DeBakey 3: monitor, surgery if progressive.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the colour change sequence in Raynaud's phenomenon?
back: White to blue to red.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare primary and secondary Raynaud's.
back: Primary: common, from drilling/vibrating equipment causing vasospasm, rare complications, no pharmacological treatment; secondary: rare, associated with collagen vascular and autoimmune diseases, has complications, DOC is CCB.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe subclavian steal syndrome and its treatment.
back: Subclavian artery (1st part) stenosis causing retrograde blood flow from the vertebral/basilar artery during upper-limb exercise, leading to syncopal attacks; treated by angioplasty.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe carotid artery stenosis: site, cause, C/f, management.
back: Most common site is the bifurcation, most common cause atherosclerosis; C/f: ipsilateral amaurosis fugax/monocular blindness, contralateral facial paralysis, arm/leg paralysis, hemianopia, dysphasia; angioplasty if >= 70% stenosis and symptomatic.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the etiology and clinical features of thoracic outlet syndrome?
back: Etiology: muscle weakness, cervical rib; features: arterial emboli (gangrene/claudication), venous occlusion (subclavian vein thrombosis), neurological (brachial plexus compression along the ulnar distribution).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Adson's test and the elevated arm stress (Roos) test.
back: Adson's: arm abduction at 30 degrees with head turn to the ipsilateral side and deep inhalation, giving a reduced/absent ipsilateral radial pulse; Roos: shoulder abduction with opening and closing the hand, giving pain and heaviness.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of AV fistulae, and the test before a dialysis fistula?
back: Traumatic, iatrogenic (most common: Cimmino/radiocephalic fistula for dialysis), and congenital; the modified Allen's test checks radioulnar patency.

subject: Surgery
front: What is a cirsoid aneurysm, and the features of a congenital AV fistula?
back: Cirsoid aneurysm: an AV malformation of the superficial temporal vessels causing a pulsatile head swelling; congenital AV fistula: pulsatile swelling, limb hypertrophy, high-output cardiac failure, Nicoladoni/Branham sign (pressing the feeding vessel reduces size and pulse and raises systolic BP); managed by embolisation.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe cystic hygroma: tissue, site, C/f, management.
back: Sequestered lymphatic tissue; most common in the posterior triangle of the neck; brilliantly transilluminant and partly compressible; managed by aspiration then surgery, with sclerotherapy for small ones.

subject: Surgery
front: What is lymphedema, and classify primary lymphedema.
back: Excessive interstitial fluid; primary (rare): congenita (0-2 yrs, M>F, Noone-Milroy syndrome) and praecox (most common, 2-35 yrs, Meig's disease).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the most common causes of secondary lymphedema?
back: Upper limb: post-mastectomy (LN clearance > SLNB); lower limb: filariasis/elephantiasis.

subject: Surgery
front: Give Brunner's classification and the gold-standard investigation of lymphedema.
back: Subclinical (no signs), Stage I (pitting edema), Stage II (non-pitting edema), Stage III (skin changes); gold standard is water plethysmography.

subject: Surgery
front: What is Stewart-Treves syndrome?
back: Angiosarcoma developing in long-standing lymphedema (after 7-8 years).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the surgical options for lymphedema.
back: Lymphovenous anastomosis (in breast cancer to prevent lymphedema), Homan's procedure (removal of tissue chunks with skin; complication skin necrosis), Thompson's reduction (buried dermal flap; complication pilonidal sinus), and Charle's procedure (worst cosmesis; full-thickness removal + split skin graft).

subject: Surgery
front: Compare STSG and FTSG by other name, layers, and donor sites.
back: STSG (Thiersch graft): epidermis & part of dermis, donor sites anterolateral thigh/buttocks; FTSG (Wolfe graft): epidermis & whole dermis, donor sites post-auricular and supra/infraclavicular skin.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare STSG and FTSG donor site handling and reuse.
back: STSG: donor site only dressed and can be reused; FTSG: donor site sutured and cannot be reused.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare secondary and primary contracture in grafts.
back: STSG: secondary contracture (when placed on the recipient bed), better graft survival; FTSG: primary contracture (immediately after harvesting, depends on dermal component), cosmetically better and more trauma-resistant.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the purpose of meshing an STSG, and what does Humby's knife do?
back: Meshing increases surface area and prevents seroma (punctate bleeding points indicate adequate thickness); Humby's knife is used to harvest STSG.

subject: Surgery
front: Name the methods and timeline of graft survival.
back: Imbibition (1-2 days), inosculation (2-4 days), and neovascularization (> 4 days).

subject: Surgery
front: What are the causes of graft failure?
back: Seroma formation (most common), infection, movement/shearing force, and a poor recipient bed (excessive granulation tissue, lack of periosteum, infected bed).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe random flaps and the Z-plasty.
back: Based on dermal vessels (e.g. V-Y plasty, Z-plasty); they help in post-burn contractures, and a 60-degree Z-plasty gives 1.75x elongation.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the rhomboid/Limberg flap used for, and what defines an axial flap?
back: The rhomboid/Limberg flap (a random flap) is used in pilonidal sinus; an axial flap is based on known blood vessels.

subject: Surgery
front: Name some axial flaps and their uses.
back: Deltopectoral flap; PMMC (pectoralis major myocutaneous, most used in head & neck surgery); Abbe-Estlander flap (angle of mouth & lip reconstruction); latissimus dorsi flap (thoracodorsal pedicle).

subject: Surgery
front: Compare TRAM and DIEP abdominal flaps.
back: TRAM (transversus rectus abdominis myocutaneous): uses muscle, with increased incisional hernia risk; DIEP (deep inferior epigastric artery perforator): muscle preserved, the best flap for breast reconstruction.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe free flaps and give two examples.
back: Disconnected from the donor site with microvascular anastomosis at the recipient site; radial artery forearm flap (head & neck, needs modified Allen's test) and free fibular flap (mandibular reconstruction).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the Mathes and Nahai Type V flap.
back: One dominant pedicle plus multiple minor pedicles; examples are pectoralis major and latissimus dorsi.

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate arterial and venous block in flap failure.
back: Arterial block: cold, pale, reduced capillary refill, decreased blood flow on pinprick; venous block: warm, congested, quick capillary refill, increased blood flow on pinprick.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the staging of bed sores.
back: Stage 1: non-blanchable erythema without breach in epidermis; Stage 2: partial-thickness loss (epidermis and dermis); Stage 3: full-thickness loss into subcutaneous tissue; Stage 4: full-thickness loss involving muscle, bone, tendon, or joint.

subject: Surgery
front: Give the frequency of bed sores (descending) and risk factors.
back: Ischium > greater trochanter > sacrum > heel > malleolus; risk factors: prolonged immobilization (constant pressure > 30 mmHg), malnutrition, maceration.

subject: Surgery
front: How are bed sores prevented and managed by grade?
back: Prevention: change position 10 minutes every 2 hours, air/water mattress; Grade I: keep dry + offloading; Grade II/III: debridement + VAC dressing; Grade IV: debridement + VAC/flap.

subject: Surgery
front: What is VAC dressing, and its uses?
back: Negative-pressure dressing (-120 to -125 mmHg) that hastens healing; used for chronic non-healing wounds, venous ulcers without slough, burn wounds without eschar, bed sores after debridement, and diabetic ulcers without osteomyelitis.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the phases of wound healing?
back: Hemostasis, inflammation, proliferative, and remodelling.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe wound strength recovery over time.
back: 10% of normal after 1 week; 70-80% (maximum) after 3 months/12 weeks; original strength is never regained.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the collagen type change in wound healing.
back: Type 3 initially, then type 1 in remodelling (type 1 : type 3 = 4 : 1).

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate primary, secondary, and tertiary intention healing.
back: Primary: clean incised wound sutured, good scar with minimal contracture; secondary: wound left open, more granulation tissue, hypertrophic scar, and contracture; tertiary: wound left open initially then sutured after a few days (delayed primary closure).

subject: Surgery
front: Differentiate keloid and hypertrophic scar.
back: Keloid: sternum/shoulder/ear lobule, dark-skinned predisposition, grows beyond the scar, doesn't subside, treated with intralesional triamcinolone; hypertrophic scar: extensor surface, more in children, stays within the scar, subsides with time/pressure, treated with silicone pads/gel.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common cleft defect, and the LAHSAL system?
back: Combined cleft lip plus palate; LAHSAL: Lip, Alveolus, Hard palate, Soft palate, Alveolus, Lip; capitals denote a complete cleft and small letters a partial cleft.

subject: Surgery
front: When is cleft lip repaired (rule of 10), and the technique?
back: Earliest at 3 months (rule of 10: 10 weeks, 10 gm% Hb, 10 lbs weight), most commonly done at 5-6 months; technique: Millard (most common), with crucial repair of the orbicularis oris.

subject: Surgery
front: When are cleft soft and hard palate repaired, and the techniques?
back: Soft palate: 3-6 months; hard palate: 9-12 months (bony growth); techniques: Wardill-Kilner, V-Y plasty/Von Langenbeck.

subject: Surgery
front: Define autograft, isograft, allograft, and xenograft.
back: Autograft: from the same person (e.g. skin graft); isograft: from an identical twin; allograft: from the same species; xenograft: from a different species.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Maastricht classification I and II.
back: I: dead on arrival (uncontrolled), organs heart valves and cornea; II: unsuccessful resuscitation (uncontrolled), organs kidney, heart valves, cornea.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Maastricht classification III, IV, and V.
back: III: anticipated cardiac arrest (controlled); IV: cardiac arrest in a brain-dead donor (controlled, all organs except heart); V: unexpected cardiac arrest in a hospital patient (uncontrolled).

subject: Surgery
front: Give the longest and shortest cold ischemia times.
back: Longest: kidney (36 hours); shortest: heart (< 3 hours).

subject: Surgery
front: What is UW solution and normothermic organ storage?
back: UW (University of Wisconsin) solution: for static cold storage at 4 degrees C, flushing out blood (preventing thrombosis) and cooling organs (reducing O2 need); normothermic storage: preserves heart/lung/liver/kidney in a physiological environment, restoring function ex-vivo and improving marginal organ utility.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the most common indications for renal transplant?
back: Adults: diabetes mellitus; children: glomerulonephritis.

subject: Surgery
front: State the extended donor criteria for renal transplant.
back: A fit donor > 60 years, or > 50 years with two or more of: death due to stroke, history of HTN, serum creatinine > 1.5 mg/dL.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe the anastomoses and site of renal transplant.
back: Ureter to bladder, renal vein to iliac vein, renal artery to iliac artery; sited in the iliac fossa.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe hyperacute rejection.
back: Dusky kidney on the table due to preformed anti-HLA antibodies; a type II hypersensitivity reaction with intravascular thrombosis on HPE.

subject: Surgery
front: Compare acute and chronic rejection.
back: Acute: immunological, reduced by effective immunosuppression (>95% 5-year, >90% 10-year graft survival); chronic: the most common type, occurring 6 months after transplant, a type IV hypersensitivity reaction.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe infection and malignancy after renal transplant.
back: Infection is maximal in the first 6 months (1st month bacterial, overall viral with CMV most common); malignancy is skin cancer (SCC) and PTLD due to EBV; the most common vascular complication is renal vein thrombosis.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the most common indications for liver transplant, and is HLA matching needed?
back: Adults: cirrhosis; children: biliary atresia; HLA matching is not mandatory (no hyperacute rejection).

subject: Surgery
front: Describe split, auxiliary, and domino liver transplants.
back: Split: segments 2+3 to a child, segments 4-8 to an adult; auxiliary: the diseased liver is not removed and the donor liver piggybacks it; domino: donor and recipient both have the same systemic disease.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the King's College criteria, and the Milan criteria for HCC?
back: King's College criteria: for acute liver failure (acetaminophen and non-acetaminophen induced); Milan criteria for transplant in HCC: one lesion <= 5 cm or three or fewer lesions <= 3 cm.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the indications for liver transplant in malignancy?
back: Children with hepatoblastoma and HCC (most common), and liver metastasis from colorectal and neuroendocrine tumors.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe berry aneurysms.
back: Occur in the circle of Willis; the most common site of rupture is the apex of the aneurysm; the most common site is the junction of the anterior communicating artery with the ACA; rupture causes SAH.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the C/f, diagnosis, and management of SAH?
back: Thunderclap headache (the worst headache of life); diagnosed by NCCT; managed with cerebral angiography then intervention (coiling or surgical clipping), with post-op nimodipine to prevent vasospasm.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the most common primary brain tumour overall, in children, and the most common brain tumour?
back: Primary overall: glioma > meningioma; in children: medulloblastoma; most common brain tumour overall: metastasis.

subject: Surgery
front: What is the IOC for CNS tumors, and a caveat?
back: MRI; PET-CT can miss brain lesions.

subject: Surgery
front: Which cancers most commonly metastasize to the cerebrum and leptomeninges?
back: Cerebrum: lung cancer (prophylactic CNS radiation in certain stages); leptomeninges: breast cancer.

subject: Surgery
front: How is brain metastasis managed?
back: Radiotherapy, surgery for solitary lesions, steroids (DOC for vasogenic edema), and intrathecal chemotherapy.

subject: Surgery
front: Describe Grade I and Grade 4 astrocytoma.
back: Grade I: pilocytic astrocytoma, the most common astrocytoma in children, presents as a mural nodule; Grade 4: glioblastoma multiforme, crosses the midline (butterfly tumour), treated with surgery then radiotherapy and oral temozolomide.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the HPE features of oligodendroglioma?
back: Chicken-wire vascularity and a fried-egg appearance.

subject: Surgery
front: Which ulcers have a sloping edge?
back: Healing ulcers and venous ulcers.

subject: Surgery
front: Which ulcers have a punched-out edge?
back: Arterial, neuropathic (trophic), and bed sore ulcers.

subject: Surgery
front: Which ulcer has an undermined edge?
back: Tubercular (TB) ulcer.

subject: Surgery
front: Which lesion gives a rolled-out, pearly white ulcer edge?
back: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC).

subject: Surgery
front: Which lesion gives a raised, everted, cauliflower ulcer edge?
back: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC).

subject: Surgery
front: What is the Hutchinson sign?
back: Pigmentation of the nail fold seen in subungual melanoma.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the eFAST (M-mode) findings in tension pneumothorax?
back: Loss of the seashore sign, with the bar code/stratosphere sign present.

subject: Surgery
front: What are the four anatomical types of hydrocele (by extent along the processus vaginalis)?
back: Vaginal hydrocele, infantile hydrocele, congenital hydrocele, and hydrocele of the cord.
